RCA: Root Cause Analysis RIS: Radiology Information System RMO: Resident Medical Officer SCGH: Sir Charles Gairdner Hospital SIPOC: Stakeholders/Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs/Outcomes, Critical to Quality SMART: Specific, Measurable, Attainable, Relevant, Timebound SMHS: South Metropolitan Health Service SOP: Standard Operating Procedure TMS: Theatre Management System TOPAS: The Open Patient Administration System TOR: Terms of Reference VOO: Voice of the Organisation VOP: Voice of the Patient VOS: Voice of the Staff WA: Western Australia WACHS: WA Country Health Service WebPAS: Patient Administration System WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 3 Executive Summary WA Health currently provides a high standard of clinical care to patients; however the demand for health services continues to rise. A number of strategic initiatives to manage this demand are currently driving reform in the WA Health System. A consistent approach is needed to ensure reform projects deliver the anticipated benefits. Clinical Service Redesign (CSR) methodology has been used by WA Health since 2007 and is a proven, rigorous, international methodology that has been tailored to the needs of WA Health using principles from 6 Sigma and LEAN as well as incorporating project and change management methodologies. CSR promotes a patient centred approach to redesign by addressing the needs and preferences of patients and examining every step of the patient journey. The methodology involves a large stakeholder group ensuring engagement and sustainability of implemented solutions. The Clinical Service Redesign Handbook has been developed as a tool to assist sites to review and redesign current service processes using the CSR methodology. The expected outcome is the identification and implementation of a range of strategies to assist with reducing waste, streamlining processes, improving service consistency and quality, and ensuring a smooth progression across services delivered throughout a patient’s journey. By undertaking a CSR process, health sites will have a better understanding of the current picture of processes within the system, and an ability to identify and undertake improvement opportunities. This CSR Guide is an overview of CSR methodology, however individual sites need to take into account local information and adapt their approach to target areas of highest need. 4 Background While the standard of health care WA Health provides to our patients is high, there are increasing demands on the system due to the ageing population, increasing rates of chronic and preventable disease, new treatments becoming available, rising health care costs, and resource and workforce constraints (Wullink, Van Houdenhoven et al. 2007). To manage current and future demand, reduce waiting times and improve the patient experience, WA Health must reform and streamline its processes. Clinical Services Redesign (CSR) is a methodology which was developed by WA Health to address these issues. This manual was developed by the Health System Improvement Unit (HSIU) and is designed to be used by service redesign managers and/ or teams who may not be familiar with service redesign or project management processes. The manual covers the planning, implementation and review of service redesign initiatives, and should be used in conjunction with training in Clinical Services Redesign run by the HSIU. The National Health Reform Agreement between Federal Government, States and Territories is driving reform activity to improve health service delivery in Western Australia. The focus of the Agreement is to improve access to elective surgery and emergency care and is underpinned by performance targets. Other policies that direct health services reform include the: ■ WA Health Clinical Services Framework 2010–2012 ■ National Partnership Agreement – Improving Services in Public Hospitals 2010–2016. This Agreement defines the National Emergency Access Target (NEAT) and the National Elective Surgery Target (NEST). Clinical Services Redesign is a process to facilitate, identify and address inefficiencies or breakdowns with relevant solutions to ensure patients move through a coordinated system and receive high quality care (Stokes 2011). CSR seeks to fundamentally redesign health care systems to ensure that each patient journey is one the patient, their family and health professionals acknowledge is safe and of the highest quality. This involves realigning systems, workforces and resources to ensure patients receive the right care, in the right place, at the right time and are supported by the right people (Gabow, Eisert et al. 2005). There are many reasons which compel health care systems to embark on services redesign. These include: ■ Continued rise in hospital costs ■ Concerns about patient safety and quality ■ Little change in the core health care processes over decades ■ Silos of care provision ■ Little application of knowledge from other industries ■ Redundancies in care delivery processes ■ Numerous patient handoffs/handovers ■ Major workforce shortages ■ Employee dissatisfaction ■ Work hour limitations. ■ Limited involvement of the patients and their families in hospital care ■ Patient dissatisfaction. Background WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 5 The objectives of clinical process reform are to: ■ Identify factors that impede patient and process flow ■ Collect and analyse evidence (including data) ■ Design appropriate solutions to address the key flow issues ■ Develop plans to guide the implementation of the agreed solutions ■ Manage the change process so that sustained outcomes can be achieved and maintained. A sustainable approach to services redesign is to use current staff and develop internal advisors to undertake redesign projects. The idea is to harness internal knowledge, and to: ■ Combine experts in proven process employment methodologies ■ Grow capability from within ■ Train leaders for the future ■ Minimise use of expensive consultants The Patient Journey The patient journey is what the patient considers their whole experience with a health care service. This is likely to involve many different staff members and health care settings. Below is an example of an unplanned, or emergency, patient pathway. Unplanned patient pathway Primary Care Community Emergency Department Inpatient Acute Care Inpatient Sub-acute Sub-acute Community Patient Discharged Acute Assessment Unit Direct Admit Benefits of Redesign The redesign of services to best utilise resources and meet the workload of a facility can produce a range of benefits including: ■ Clinical Performance – Reduced morbidity and mortality, improved patient outcomes – Improved predictability and equity of access to services – Better provision of training in specialty areas ■ Service Management – Improved service utilisation – Reduction in cancellations – Reduction in delays for patients – Reduction in length of stay ■ Resource Utilisation – Improved access to specialty services – Improved use of radiology and pathology Background6 ■ Health Service Management – Allows an area-wide viewpoint, creating an overview of services and delivery methods and flow occurring within a health service area – Ensuring the best allocation and use of resources and funds across a health service Process improvement seeks to provide sustainable solutions to the issue of providing services to an increasing number of people within limited resources. It is about doing things better and smarter and therefore maximising the time, materials, people and resources we have. Background WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 7 Clinical Service Redesign This guide has been developed to assist health services in undertaking a redesign process. It is a step by step guide to leading a project team through the phases and core tasks required to undertake reform. WA Health CSR methodology uses tools from Six Sigma and LEAN methodologies, as well as incorporating project and change management principles. The CSR methodology follows a five step cyclic improvement process known as DMAIC: Define, Measure, Analyse, Improve and Control. DMAIC redesigns health service provision using a data based diagnostic process to identify real problems and delays. The process involves capturing the voice of the staff, patient and organisation to identify and validate problems, and develop sustained solutions to improve the patient journey whilst reducing inefficiencies in the process. The DMAIC process has four overarching ‘layers’: project management, stakeholder consultation and involvement, change and risk management and governance and leadership. Figure 1 illustrates the stages of DMAIC and the outer layers. Table 1 outlines the key activities for each stage, with estimated timeframes. It should be noted that the allocated timeframes are only indicative and may vary with the size of the reform scope selected. Figure 1: DMAIC Process Background8 Table 1: DMAIC key activities Phase Key activities Timeframe (example only) Project Initiation Establishment of project lead, clinical lead, executive sponsor and project team; define the scope; develop governance structures utilise project management tools 2–4 weeks Define Define the problem or improvement opportunity; map the voice of the staff, patient and organisation; and identify the projects’ critical to quality requirements 2–4 weeks Measure Measure size and impact of the issues and delays captured in Define by measuring and collecting data. 5–7 weeks Analyse Identify and validate root causes 5–7 weeks Improve Develop a vision; design, plan and build the solution; and implement the solution 8–10 weeks Control Develop reporting structures and processes; conduct formal scheduled reviews; plan for succession; continuous improvement Ongoing How to Use this Handbook This guide presents a step by step guide to undertaking a redesign process in any health facility. The guide is intended to be used as a supporting document to the CSR training provided by HSIU. When undertaking reform projects, it is essential that each step is sequenced in the order set out in this document. While it may seem easier or quicker to skip a particular step, doing so may result in solutions that are not evidenced based, do not have engagement, or are not sustainable. There are extensive tools and examples of how to use the tools to assist with each stage and step of the process – these can be found at the end of each section. Not all these tools have to be used, and they can each be adapted to fit with individual reform activities. The most important thing is to progress through the consecutive sections and avoid jumping ahead. This may jeopardise the success and sustainability of your reform work. Background WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 9 Project Initiation The first step of the process involves preliminary project planning. The project initiation stage is about developing the foundations of your reform project before heading into the DMAIC cycle. It involves the project set-up, structure, resources required, scope and work plan. It also involves investigation of your site’s current state, reform activity and baseline data to determine problem areas that need to be addressed. This will help outline and clarify the ‘what’ and ‘why’ of undertaking a reform activity. When undertaking your review it is important to consider the entire patient journey within the area you are reforming. This section addresses a range of aspects that will continue to impact on the project throughout each stage, such as stakeholder engagement and risk management – the outer rings of the DMAIC diagram on page 6. Key Activities: ■ Identify the project team and develop governance structure ■ Gather and review baseline data ■ Identify key areas for reform ■ Develop project charter ■ Develop project documents Overview of Key Activities: Step 1: Identify the project team and develop Governance structure When a services redesign project takes place, a project team must be formed. Having the commitment and involvement from the right people at the start can help ensure the successful running of the project. Executive Sponsor The project requires an Executive Sponsor at site level. This person should have a level of governance or line management responsibility over at least part of the project scope. The responsibilities of the Executive Sponsor include: ■ Providing strategic and operational direction and leadership for the project ■ Overseeing the internal governance ■ Monitoring project risks and assist in resolving high level site specific issues ■ Overseeing stakeholder management activities and assist with performance management if necessary ■ Monitoring project outcomes against project targets ■ Overseeing solution development and implementation ■ Providing advocacy and support for the members of the project Project Initiation10 Project Lead All reform projects should have an appointed Project Lead. It is recommended this position be an additional resource. The amount of FTE required will vary depending on the size of the reform activity being undertaken. The FTE should be allocated for the duration of the project to ensure reform is fully embedded in system activities throughout all of the DMAIC stages. The responsibilities of the Project Lead may include: ■ Overall project management ■ Ensuring timely delivery of project deliverables, including managing timelines and resources ■ Ensuring regular, comprehensive reporting; and that issues resulting from reports are addressed or escalated in an appropriate manner ■ Providing support and guidance to team members around the project management and change management tasks and activities associated with the project ■ Providing advocacy for the project at a site level and act as a touch-point for staff ■ Represent the project at meetings Clinical Leads/Champions A Clinical Lead or Champion should be identified for each project to assist in promoting medical engagement and provide clinical input for decision making. Depending on the scale of the project it is recommended this position be an additional resource and the site should attempt to allocate dedicated FTE for at least the diagnostic and implementation phases of the project. The responsibilities of the Clinical Lead include: ■ Acting as a clinical leader for the project ■ Promoting and facilitating medical engagement in the project ■ Providing clinical advocacy and direction for the project ■ Promote clinical guidance and participate in strategy development for the project at the facility ■ Liaising with stakeholders and represent the project at meetings or other sessions requiring clinical leadership Project Team Each area of reform needs a project team to guide and execute the design and implementation of the specific project. The selection of members of the team is crucial; the project team should be: ■ Multidisciplinary ■ Represent all parts of the process ■ Motivated and engaged ■ Have representatives from both management and clinical staff ■ Have capacity to contribute dedicated time outside their normal duties The responsibilities of the Project Teams include: ■ Undertake tasks identified by the Project and Clinical Leads and work on assigned activities as required. This may involve communication with stakeholders, acting as a leader amongst colleagues (as a ‘change champion’) and facilitating engagement, promoting key project messages, project planning and decision making Project Initiation WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 11 ■ Act as a representative of the project and provide information flow between the Project Team and relevant clinical and non-clinical stakeholder groups There may be additions or changes to the above responsibilities that site executives want their project members to undertake. Any changes to an individual’s responsibilities, tasks or duties should be clearly discussed, documented and agreed on at the beginning of the project. Develop Project Governance Structure It is recommended that project teams are guided by their site Reform Managers to identify any existing Governance Structures or work towards developing one. It is recommended that the governance structure is demonstrated visually. See the end of this section for an example of a project governance structure. In developing the Governance Structure thought should be given to: ■ Roles and responsibilities ■ Who the decision-making group is (i.e. a working group to which issues can be escalated and which can give approval to progress) ■ Frequency of project team meetings ■ Frequency of reporting to the working group and the Executive Sponsor ■ Method of reporting and responsibility for reporting Working Group Larger scale projects may consider the need to establish a working group or steering committee within the Governance Structure who are in a position to oversee all service redesign and implementation projects, with an organisation wide view. A working group can take a step back and look at the project from a different perspective and can ask difficult questions about progress and performance. They may see things that the project manager has overlooked and can also provide support by providing contacts and insights that help get things done, and by providing “political cover”. The members of this committee will also be fundamental in assisting with the cultural change within the organization. Rather than creating another group to provide oversight of redesign, project teams may also consider tying their governance structure into existing meetings at the site. Depending on the size and importance of the project, this may include meeting such as Nursing Managers meetings or Hospital Executive Group meetings. Responsibilities include: ■ Provide a framework for services redesign and implementation ■ Make recommendations about the priority areas for reform (and recommendations about work not to progress at points in time) ■ Evaluate design solutions presented by business and clinical leaders and make recommendations ■ Evaluate change management plans and make recommendations ■ Monitor the progress of reform Project Initiation12 Identify the Need for CSR or Project Management Training for Project Staff A thorough working knowledge of the CSR methodology is required to undertake this type of project. CSR training is available for project teams. Contact the Health System Improvement Unit (HSIU) for further information. For more information on this training, please contact the Health System Improvement Unit: Tel: (08) 9222 6461 Email: HSIU@health.wa.gov.au http://www.health.wa.gov.au/HRIT/home/ An understanding of project management is also required to undertake the process. No single project management methodology is recommended. Training is available through a variety of sources such as Department of Health Training and Development, and other external sources. Step 2: Gather and Review Baseline Data The project team must identify what baseline data is required to gain an understanding of the problem underlining their project. This may include information on service demand, activity, and capacity. It may be worthwhile to gather additional site specific information on staffing allocations and service availability. Take the time to understand your facility’s current service models and activities across the different aspects of service delivery, this will help to identify potential starting points for the project. Data can be obtained from a variety of sources, some examples are: ■ Requests for data can be submitted to their Data Analyst at site or the Health System Improvement Unit (HSIU). ■ The performance reporting branch of Performance Activity and Quality produces many reports that may be of use including NEAT Reporting, Elective Surgery and Outpatient Reporting ■ HSIU produces reports on both NEAT and Elective Services Reform. To access these reports an access request form must be completed, authorised and returned to HSIU. You can get this access request form from the data analysts at the HSIU ■ SMHS, NMHS and WACHS have performance reporting groups ■ Some health services and hospitals have a group overseeing reform and redesign initiatives who may have access to certain reports. ■ Hospital-based Safety Quality and Performance Groups. These groups often have access to data and information that may be useful in the reform process. Some of these groups have data analysts Note that detailed collection and analysis of particular data elements will occur at a later stage. Step 3: Identify Key Areas for Reform As a site you may choose to approach reform in stages. For example, initially selecting a particular area within your site to focus on for redesign, and prioritising areas needing attention first. Project Initiation WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 13 Alternatively, you may consider starting with particular areas within the hospital such as either emergency or elective surgery; or mapping across all service areas by particular specialties. Irrespective of how your site chooses to begin their reform, each area or stream will need to be addressed for each of the different components that make up the patient journey, across all specialties. It may be helpful to construct a high level plan to prioritise the order for subsequent reform areas/streams. When choosing the initial reform stage it is also important to consider interrelated areas, as changes you make in one area have the potential to flow on to others. For example, if you focus on emergency theatres as your initial reform area, this is likely to have an impact on elective surgeries, and potentially a flow-on effect on the Emergency Department and Intensive Care or High Dependency Units. To validate the improvement opportunities, the first planned stage should be discussed with key staff in the relevant areas to gain an insight into how the data matches ground level operations. These discussions will help you assess the willingness for change in particular areas and may help in identifying change champions. Step 4: Develop a Project Charter A project charter is a statement of the scope and objectives for a project. The purpose of the project charter is to document the reasons for undertaking the project, objectives and constraints, identify in and out of scope items, and state the key outcomes expected from the project. The project charter is also useful in identifying what (if any) resources are available for the project. It may be updated at a later stage once the scope is clarified. A site may develop several project charters to represent the different reform streams they are undertaking (e.g. one for theatre reform and one for outpatient reform). If this is the case, any relationship or interdependencies between the projects should be documented when considering each project scope. See an example of a project charter at the end of this section. Tools such as a SIPOC (Stakeholders/Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs/Outcomes, Critical to Quality) can help to define a process and who and what is to be involved before beginning a project, and can be a useful resource to gain clarity around project scope. See an example of a SIPOC at the end of this section. Step 5: Develop Project Documents Developing other project documents, which expand on the project charter, will assist by giving more detail of the tasks required to ensure project success. These documents include: ■ Stakeholder identification map ■ Stakeholder communication plan ■ Risk management plans, ie – Risk/ Probability Mapping Matrix – Risk Management Log ■ Gantt Chart (project planning tool) See Appendix A to read more about Stakeholder Management Project Initiation14 Stakeholder identification map If people are involved and understand what is happening within the project, they will have a greater sense of ownership and involvement, making the project more likely to be a success. Aim to have appropriate representation within your stakeholder plan of all disciplines and services that will be affected by the reform process. Stakeholders can be individuals, groups, people with direct interest and accountability for implementation, people who will be involved or affected intermittently, or people who have little involvement but are interested. A stakeholder is anyone who has a vested interest in the outcome of the project, for example: ■ Executive team ■ Senior Clinicians ■ Nurses ■ Allied Health ■ Community Services ■ Patients and carers ■ Non Government organisations ■ Admin support staff ■ WA Health ■ Unions ■ Patient support staff They are people who will judge and contribute to the success or failure of the project. See Stakeholder Identification Mapping tool at the end of this section. Stakeholder communication plan Developing a stakeholder communication plan involves determining the needs, wants, concerns, authority, and positioning of each stakeholder according to the level of influence and impact they could have on the project. The success of any project involving people depends on the project team’s ability to engage and manage the key groups and individuals who have a vested interest in the outcome of the project. See a stakeholder communication plan at the end of this section. Risk management plans A risk is an unforeseen event or activity that can impact on a project’s progress, result or outcome. Every project has risks associated with it. The success of a project depends on the project manager’s ability to manage and treat these risks, so that they no longer pose a threat to the project’s outcomes. Wherever possible, plans should be developed to reduce or eliminate risks, and these risks should be monitored throughout the course of the project. See a Risk/ Probability Mapping Matrix and a Risk Management Log at the end of this section. See Appendix B to read more about Communication Management and techniques See Appendix C to read more about Risk management Project Initiation WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 15 Change Management Principles Staff engagement or resistance to change has the potential to make or break your reform activity. Taking the time to consider how your facility will approach change management, the process of transitioning your organisation from the current state to the future desired state, is an essential pre-project planning activity. Assessing the site’s readiness for change, identifying enablers and barriers (staff and processes) and communicating the need for change are important factors in managing change. Gantt Chart A Gantt Chart is a tool used to map out timelines for the project schedule. Each stage and element that makes up the project, including start and end dates and key milestones are represented. Gantt Charts are a key tool to keep track of what activities need to be achieved and by when. It is also useful in representing interdependencies between tasks (for example needing to recruit a Project Lead before being able to start a project scope) or those tasks which have no relationship and can occur concurrently. See a Gantt chart at the end of this section. There are multiple tools that are available to help with setting up your project – additional templates and help is available from the HSIU team. Contact: Tel: (08) 9222 6461 Email: HSIU@health.wa.gov.au http://www.health.wa.gov.au/HRIT/home/ All project documents should be continually reviewed and updated throughout the course of the project. See Appendix D to read more about Change Management Hints and Tips Project Initiation16 Project Initiation: Tools and Resources Project Governance Structure Why use it? Governance provides direction, guidance, and a critical review of the project and its progress. Who does it? The Project Team. What does it do? Outlines the governance structure of the project team so that the lines of responsibility are clear to everyone. Terms of Reference (TOR) are also defined. How do you do it? The governance structure composition depends heavily on the scope of the project. However, regardless of scope, one person must assume the role of project lead. As the service reform project will be effecting change at a local level, the membership must include staff with fundamental knowledge of core processes. Example of a simple governance chart: HSIU Advisor Hospital Executive Group Membership: ED, DCS, DON, Area Directors Executive Sponsor xxx Project Team Team Lead: xxx Team Members: xxx TOR: Accountability Communication Project Initiation WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 17 Project charter: Why use it? The project charter assists to give authorisation to a project as, once it is endorsed by executive, the project is a legitimate and approved part of a health site’s core business. Who does it? The project team develops the charter, however for the project to commence it must be signed off by Executive. What does it do? The charter is generally a static document that assists with defining roles and responsibilities, outlines the project objectives, identifies the main stakeholders, and defines the authority of the project leader. It serves as a reference point for the duration of the project, as it can be used as a baseline document to keep team meetings on track, or as a short summary which project team members can use when discussing the project with stakeholders. How do you do it? Complete a project charter template, considering the points below. The opportunity statement should NOT….. ■ attempt to identify the root cause of the problem ■ define the scope too narrowly or too broadly ■ make assumptions that cannot be supported with data ■ focus on things that will not improve patient satisfaction or add value ■ contain or identify a solution The project goals/objectives should NOT…. ■ be too general ■ describe the solution ■ point fingers or blame This acronym is a checklist to ensure that the charter is effective and thorough: S Specific Does it address a real problem? M Measurable Are we able to measure the problem, establish a baseline, and set targets for improvement? A Attainable Is the goal achievable? R Relevant Does it relate to an organisation objective? T Time bound Have we set an achievable date for completion? Opportunity statement How will your project address these described problems? What are the benefits that will be gained by implementing the project - include, a brief description of the main benefit anticipated and the measure (how will you know you have achieved it). Project Goals/Objectives Should address these questions: ■ How will the teams’ success be measured? ■ What specific parameters will be measured? ■ What will be the tangible, “hard” deliverables/results? ■ What will be the intangible, “soft” deliverables/results? ■ What will be the timetable for delivery of results? Each goal should be SMART i.e. Specific, Measurable, Attainable, Relevant and Timebound. This will enable progress towards achieving the Project’s objectives to be regularly reviewed by the Project Sponsor. Project scope: Should address these questions: ■ What are the starting and ending points of the process? ■ What components of the organisation are included? ■ What, if anything, is outside the project boundaries? ■ Under what constraints must the team work? In scope: Out of scope: Project Initiation WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 19 Governance: It could include: ■ A description of key Project roles and responsibilities, and if relevant the names of the individuals fulfilling these roles ■ A Governance Structure diagram for the Project ■ A description of how the Structure will operate i.e. Terms of Reference for any Project Boards or Groups; frequency and structure of meetings Project reporting ■ List the main KPIs that will be monitored and reported on/frequency of reporting/ target audience for the reports Stakeholder Mapping: This section should identify stakeholders and record their information needs along with methods of communication to be used and the appropriate timing. Communication planning helps to ensure that everyone who needs to be informed about project activities and results gets the required information. This information will form part of the Stakeholder Mapping Log. Risk Management: This section should be updated with information about the risks, their analysis, countermeasures and status. This information will form part of the Risk Management Log. Project Plan: This section should be updated with the high-level Project Plan, including key project activities; key deliverables: timelines and milestones; Once finalised and approved, this project plan will be one of the key control documents for the project and progress against it/variances from it should be actively monitored and reported. Project Initiation20 SIPOC tool Why use it? SIPOC is an acronym for Stakeholders(or Suppliers), Inputs, Process, Outputs(or Outcomes) and Critical to Quality requirements. Whenever you are planning a service improvement activity, it’s important to get a high-level understanding of the scope of the process first. A SIPOC chart helps the team to agree on the boundaries of what they will be working on. Who does it? The project team. What does it do? Helps to define a process and who and what is to be involved before beginning a project, and can be a useful resource to gain clarity around project scope. It provides a structured way to discuss the process and get consensus on what it involves during the planning a process mapping session, or any other group facilitation session. How do you do it? 1. Define the scope of the process by determining what is in and out of scope. 2. Identify three to five high level process steps. 3. Identify stakeholders involved in the process. This will provide a basis for determining who will attend sessions. 4. Define the inputs to the process. These are the things that trigger the process and include everything other than people needed in the process. 5. Define the outputs or outcomes of the process. This is the required result of the process. 6. Determine the Critical to Quality requirements. Consider what is important for patients and staff. If people are involved and understand what is happening within the project, they will have a greater sense of ownership and involvement, making the project more likely to be a success. Who does it? The project team develops the stakeholder map. It is important the whole team contributes to its development to ensure that no stakeholder is left out. What does it do? There are two main benefits of completing a stakeholder analysis: ■ Identifies groups and individuals – Provides a clear picture of all groups and individuals that need to be involved and helps to reduce the negative impact on the project due to uninvolved stakeholders. ■ Commitment levels - Identifies the stakeholders’ current commitment levels. This highlights the investment of time required to achieve the required level of commitment. How do you do it? Create a list of groups of stakeholders and record details about their importance and engagement with the project. Think about who is involved with each activity, whether they are directly involved or are affected by the outcome. Effective communication is central to all projects. It is critical to identify all the stakeholders who will be affected by a project and understand their communication needs, to allow the project to achieve its objectives. Communications management helps to ensure that there is communication with all stakeholders using the most appropriate methods. Who does it? Assign specific project team members to be responsible for stakeholder communication. What does it do? The purpose is to ensure that there is an open channel of communication with stakeholders, and they have someone who is communicating with them about the project on a frequent basis. A communication plan assists to systematically plan, implement, monitor, and revise all channels of communication. How do you do it? When filling out the sections of the Stakeholder Communication plan, consider the following: ■ Engagement aims (what is the message?) Weekly status reports, project executive meetings, team meetings, interviews, working parties, newsletters/ posters, emails. ■ Timing (within the project) At which stages of the project does the stakeholder need to be communicated with? ■ Frequency (how often?) Daily, weekly; or as requested? ■ Responsibility (to be actioned by whom?) Which member of the project team will be responsible for that stakeholders’ communication management? Handbook | A Training Guide 29 Risk probability/ Mapping Matrix Why use it? A risk probability/ mapping matrix is a good tool to keep track of risks and how strategies for mitigation are progressing, and noting any changes to the likelihood or impact of the risks on the project. Who does it? How risks are identified and who will be involved depends on whether the area being redesigned is a specific ward/team area or at a more strategic, organisational level.  It’s useful to involve others in identifying risk as this gives different perspectives on the same situation. What does it do? Provides a clear picture of all risks associated with a project so that they can be eliminated, minimised or managed appropriately. How do you do it? Brainstorm risks with all appropriate project team members and stakeholders, and assign their probability and impact on the table below. This should be a live document, with further risks that are identified throughout the DMAIC stages added over the whole life of the project. Project Gantt Chart Why use it? Gantt charts are a useful way to plan and allocate time and resources to get a project done. Who does it? The whole team needs to contribute to the Gantt chart to ensure it is realistic within the constraints of the project; but the project manager must have ultimate sign off. What does it do? It allows the project team to track project progress over time to ensure the project is completed on time and within resource constraints. How do you do it? Tasks must be sequenced along the project timeline in a way that meets the ultimate deadlines, and takes into account the time and resources acquired to do them. One way of accomplishing this is to use a project management software package, like Microsoft Project. The sequence of tasks is also affected by task dependency. Task dependencies show what need to be done before a given task and what then needs to follow it. Different types of dependencies include: ■ Physical limitations or constraint relationships – for example, there may only be one person with a specific skill that can complete a particular task ■ Logical limitations – for example a prototype must be built before it can be tested Finalising the schedule In order to balance the schedule, it may be necessary to consider: ■ Deadlines: Extending or negotiating final deadlines ■ Human Resources: increasing the quantity or calibre of the HR utilised in the project ■ Budget: Increasing the budget if possible ■ Task Duration: Checking to see if there are any task durations that can be comfortably shortened ■ Task dependencies: Move any tasks that to not have any dependencies to take place parallel to other tasks (remember to consider HR here) ■ Reviewing tasks: Scale down the project – if it is not realistic to meet the delivery deadline, and there is no more budget, sometimes there is no choice but to scale back on what the project will achieve. See the Director General’s Division intranet site for more about Project Management Project Initiation36 Monitor and review the schedule Managing a project also involves managing it against the baseline – the schedule– and responding to deviations as they happen. To ensure that the outcome of the project is as intended, monitor progress as work ensues. Be in communication with the members of the team regularly and often. Ensure that there are clear reports of how things are going (maintaining an open, blameless environment where communication is encouraged is advisable); and that there is consistent communication with the rest of the team on how the project is tracking overall. Involve the team in the problem solving process when something deviates from the schedule, in either an informal or formal capacity, and document any solutions reached. Tips for dealing with unanticipated issues: ■ Identify where goals may not be met and ensure that solutions are generated before problems become unrecoverable ■ Assess whether there are any schedule changes required and who needs to be informed ■ Recognise when constraints may be violated eg. Budgets being exceeded, and who needs to be informed ■ Cross check what has been done to date against what was expected (the original ‘baseline’ schedule) ■ Determine what the estimated time is to completion for uncompleted tasks Project Initiation WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 37Example of a Gantt Chart (using Microsoft Project) Project Initiation38 Example of a Gantt Chart (using Microsoft Excel) Project Initiation WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 39 Define Define is the first step in the DMAIC redesign process and involves clearly outlining the problem or improvement opportunity. Documents from the Project Initiation phase will be finalised early on in Define, once the improvement opportunity is fully understood and validated. During this phase information from patients, staff, and the organisation is captured to determine the critical requirements of the project outcomes. A high level process map of the ’as-is’ process - along with issues and delays - is also completed. It is important to include the Voice of the Patient/ Staff in the redesign journey and this should be built into every stage of the project. Key Activities: ■ Map the process(es) and capture Voice of the Staff ■ Conduct a waste audit ■ Capture Voice of the Patient ■ Capture Voice of the Organisation ■ Translate into Critical to Quality requirements ■ Identify and act on quick wins Overview of Key Activities With the project plan set, the scope defined and the project management documents developed, it is important to advocate and communicate the need for change to your key stakeholders. Convincingly explaining why the change needs to occur and making it as appealing as possible to all relevant stakeholders will help engage staff in the process. This will lead to better participation and willingness for change further down the track. Step 1: Map the Process and Capture Voice of the Staff Map the Process A process mapping workshop involves gathering key staff who are involved with the particular process that is being redesigned, and using their input to map out each step in the work process and any associated issues, delays or barriers. This is a vital aspect of service redesign as it gathers key staff together in one room to learn from each other; initiates them to the redesign process, and gives them an opportunity to provide input to the changes that are about to happen. See the end of this section for further tools and examples of how to conduct a process mapping workshop. See Appendix D to read more about Change Management Define40 Conduct a waste audit In addition to completing a process map, it may also be beneficial to conduct a waste audit. This identifies areas where resources could be used more efficiently. Identifying wastes will complement issues identified during the process mapping sessions. See the end of this section for a tool on how to identify different types of wastes. Collect Additional Voice of the Staff Collecting Voice of the staff involves capturing staff opinion of the specific area of service delivery on which you are focusing. This can include areas of delay, waste, inefficiencies, and potential solutions for things that are currently not working well within the service area. The Process Mapping Workshop will capture many issues or concerns from staff, but this may only be indicative of the people in the room (or even just the most vocal person in the room). You might wish to expand the scope to include the views of other staff in the reform area. This can be captured by staff surveys, focus groups or interviews. This can be a useful way of validating the information you have already gleaned from the process mapping method but can also provide you with additional useful information. See the section on qualitative data for further information. See the end of this section for an example of a staff survey. Step 2: Capture Voice of the Patient To ensure a patient focused strategy is used in your reform process it is essential to know what is critical to patients – what they value most within their health care journey. There are several methods available to capture this information, including proactive and reactive methods of information collection. Proactive Voice of the Patient involves actively seeking out information from the patient relating to the reform area. Methods can include: ■ Interviews - can be completed while the patient is in hospital or post discharge. WA Health has existing templates that can be adapted to suit your target group ■ Focus Groups - generally completed post discharge ■ Surveys – can be written and given to patients (e.g. in an outpatient clinic) or sent out via mail. Surveys can also be completed verbally via telephone or in person. Surveys can generally be completed at any point in the patient journey – inpatient, outpatient or post discharge ■ Observations – this may involve moving through an area of review (for example walking through an outpatient clinic and seeing what is happening in each room) or standing in one position and watching what happens around you (for example sitting in a waiting room to capture waiting times) ■ Tag ons – this process involves following the patient through a part of their journey. There are different reasons for doing this, for example validating a process map by following the flow of documentation and processes; tracking a patient to determine their emotional journey and how they feel at various stages of a process; or to capture baseline data that is not currently collected in existing measures Proactive Voice of the Patient requires consideration of when to initially approach the patient and when to capture information throughout their journey. Speaking with an acutely unwell patient or approaching a stressed patient just before surgery is unlikely to get a positive response. Define WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 41 Reactive Voice of the Patient involves reviewing already captured information relating to the reform area. Information sources can include: ■ Patient complaints ■ DoH survey data – such as the Patient Evaluation of Health Services ■ Hospital patient feedback forms You may choose to use a variety of methods to ensure you collect an adequate cross section of the client group and have included patients who have had a positive experience and those who have not. A Voice of the Patient Toolkit is available to assist with undertaking this process, available at http://www.health.wa.gov.au/hrit/home/. Step 3: Capture Voice of the Organisation Voice of the organisation can be gathered by speaking with the executive sponsor and other high level staff within your organisation and by reviewing strategic documents such as the organisation’s mission, vision or value statements. This will help identify what the organisation considers as crucial to offering a quality service and assess if the project aligns with the organisation’s priorities and strategic direction. Step 4: Translate into Critical to Quality Requirements The information gathered from Process Mapping, and Voice of the Staff should provide some key themes. These themes can be translated into ‘Critical to Quality requirements’- the things which are considered essential requirements in offering a particular service. Review your information for Voice of the Staff and pick out the key messages (usually between 3-6 main themes), then repeat this process for Voice of the Patient and for Voice of the Organisation. Themes which overlap between patients, staff and the organisation are Critical to Quality requirements for the whole project. Critical to Quality requirements help to define the boundaries of acceptable service/product/time/cost for improvements and assist in guiding the focus for solution generation and design. See the end of this section for an example of a Critical to Quality framework. Step 5: Identify and Act on Quick Wins Throughout the Process Mapping Workshops, and in capturing Voice of the Patient and Staff, some easy to fix issues may be raised. While you are not yet at the stage of determining solutions for your larger problems, it is important to deal with these quick fixes now. These issues tend to be small, may only affect certain areas, cost little or no money to make a change and can be completed quickly (for example, changing a standard phone to a cordless phone to enable the Shift Nurse Coordinator to leave the desk area and move around the ward). These easy fixes can have a big impact on engaging people early on in the project process and assists to build relationships for some of the other, larger changes to come. Define42 Ensure you have captured the voice of all staff in your process mapping. Don’t leave anyone out as this process is a key way of getting interest and buy in to your project. If people are unable or unwilling to come to a Process Mapping Workshop, try to engage them either by putting up the Process Map in a common area and encouraging others to review and comment, or meeting with individuals to discuss the Process Map document and gain their responses. Before moving on to Measure, have you … 4 Completed validated process maps? 4 Process mapped issues & opportunities? 4 Captured baseline Voice of the Staff and Patient? 4 Established Critical to Quality (CTQ) requirements? 4 Identified and remedied quick wins? Hints and Tips Define WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 43 Define: Tools and Resources Conducting a process mapping workshop: Why use it? A Process Mapping Workshop is a good way of gathering all of the key staff who are involved in a particular process and stepping out exactly what is involved for each stage of a patient’s journey. Who does it? This tool is designed to be used in a workshop situation comprising of stakeholders representing the area within the scope of the project. The session is run by the project team. What does it do? Process mapping in a group gives the opportunity for all staff to understand the role each team member plays and the contributions others make, as well as highlighting issues associated with the different stages of a process. How do you do it? Preparation Several process mapping sessions may be required to capture the various components that make up one patient journey (again this will vary depending on your project scope). Identify the Scope of what needs to be process mapped, including the: 1. Patient Group ■ Start Point of the journey you want to map ■ End Point of the journey you want to map 2. Brainstorm and validate the SIPOC with relevant manager (see Step 4 in Project Initiation) 3. Set a Date/Time/Venue that is convenient for most participants, to ensure maximum attendance. 4. Invite all stakeholders (based on SIPOC) and get their commitment to attend 5. Plan who’s going to: ■ Facilitate ■ Scribe ■ “Park” Issues (see step 11) 6. Equipment required for the session: Brown paper Blue-tac Sticky tape Post-it notes (different colours) Whiteboard markers Dot stickers Flip charts Flip chart holder Introduction 7. Clearly write the title of the process at top of the brown paper, and the start and end points on at either end. 8. OPTION: a) Assign a colour to each role and stick the colored post-it note up on the side of the board, with the role written next to it in capitals Define44 Figure 3: Process map – colour coded Title of process Nursing First step of process Last step of process Clerical staff Medical Allied Health b) Assign a row (ie Swim lane) to each role. Each step in the process is placed into the corresponding row Figure 4: Process map – Swim lanes Title of process Nursing First step of process Last step of process Clerical staff Medical Allied Health Define WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 45 ■ Welcome and Introduction: Thank everyone for their time; introduce yourself and your role of the team ■ What? – Inform the group of the scope of the process map (ie start and end points) and indicate it is a map of the ‘current status’/’as-is’ process and not what the desired journey should look like. – Indicate the level of detail required at each step – Reinforce that the focus is on identifying problems NOT solutions. The objective is to capture delays, waiting times and issues in the process. ■ Why? – To map the entire patient journey – staff often only see a small component of this journey. – It is important to understand the role of other staff and departments and how they contribute to the patient journey. ■ Ground rules: – The session should be in a safe environment, no blame – respect diversity and differences of opinion – Focus on the patient journey and what happens 80% of the time, e.g. what has the largest impact, causes major delays etc Session structure 9. Ask the group what the first step in the process is. eg: patient presents at hospital 10. The facilitator walks the group through the main branch of the process first. The scribe writes each step on a post-it note which follows a line across the brown paper to represent how the journey progresses from the start point to the end. If using coloured post-it notes for different roles, it is clear to see which role is involved at every step. If using swim lanes, each step should be written in the row that corresponds to the role involved in that particular step. 11. When someone raises an issue the Issues Scribe should number and record them on a flip chart. The corresponding number is written on the process map at the step where the issue occurs. 12. If someone raises a solution- capture it by writing on a flip chart titled ‘Car-Park’. This ensures the stakeholders are satisfied that their idea has been recorded however allows the facilitator to move on. 13. Once you have gone through the main branch- ask for variation- ( eg: times of day/ weekend etc) 14. Go through the whole journey, prompting if necessary, until the end point is reached. 15. When the main branch is complete, go back over the minor branches, so relevant detail and any issues are captured. To conclude 16. Thank the staff for their input 17. Explain what happens next: e.g ‘we’ll come back to you if we have questions after we look at all the information’ 18. Enter the map into a Visio version (see the next two pages for an example of this); this makes it easy to send to staff for validation if they were not able to attend the session; as well as give the opportunity for staff who were there to make additions. *Note that people may not have access to computers so check and print before distributing. With your redesign team look at the process map. ■ Discuss how the process can be simplified; which steps can be modified or deleted? 20. Prioritise the issues identified and action plan how you are going to tackle them. 21. Review the key issues and areas for improvement that were identified. Note areas where there are clusters of issues – this is obviously a step in the process that is most in need of attention. 22. Theme the issues into categories 23. Define50 Lean waste audit – worktime Why use it? To ensure that all potential wastes in a system are identified and addressed. Who does it? This tool is designed to be used in a workshop situation comprising of stakeholders representing the area most likely impacted by change What does it do? WORKTIME is a Lean Improvement tool which helps teams doing redesign work to identify and then minimise or eliminate waste in a system to make it more efficient. How do you do it? Using each component of WORKTIME (below); identify as many possible wastes associated with a process. Document these; identify quick wins which could easily and quickly reduce waste; and use this to inform your strategies to redesign a process. Define WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 51 WORKTIME Table template W Waiting: For people, signatures, and / or information is waste. This is ‘low hanging fruit’ which is easy to reach and ripe for the taking ■ Waiting in ED to be seen, waiting to be admitted, waiting for test results ■ Excessive signatures or approvals ■ Dependency on others to complete tasks O Overproduction: producing work prior to it being requires is waste and is the greatest of all wastes ■ Meds/documents prepared early to suit staff schedule ■ Testing/reporting too early to suit schedule ■ Producing more services than required / reports no one needs or reads ■ Batch processing R Rejects: Refers to all processing required creating a defect or mistake and the additional work required to correct it ■ Adverse Events (sentinel events, medication errors) – Wrong or missing information ■ Cancellations ■ Forwarding partial documentation to the next process ■ Lost files or records K Knowledge/Staff Utilisation: is a result of not placing people where they can (and will use their knowledge, skills, and abilities to the fullest ■ Not using staff idea, not maximising staff time on core tasks ■ Project deadlines not being met ■ Staff assignments not evenly balanced due to lack of cross-training or certification ■ Inadequate performance management system T Transport: The extra or unneeded time element associated with the delivery of work to a process ■ Transporting patients prematurely or unnecessarily moving them around ■ Moving samples, specimens, documents, equipment early/late or to the wrong location ■ Delivering documents that are not required I Inventory: Work piles, excessive supplies, and excessive signature requirements are waste ■ Duplicate supplies in excess of normal usage, running out of supplies ■ Files awaiting tasks completion or approval by others ■ Extra or out-dated manuals, newsletters or magazines. Obsolete files and equipment ■ Insufficient cross-training of staff M Motion: Any movement of people, paper, and/ or electronic exchanges that does not add value is waste ■ Searching for people (staff/ patients) ■ Searching for documents (paper or computer files) ■ Hand-carrying paper work to another process ■ Searching for poorly located supplies E Execution: Putting more work or effort into the work required by internal or external customers is waste, or doing the steps in the wrong order, ■ Too many steps or checks/ Entering repetitive data/ Steps in the wrong order ■ Duplicating tests, reports or information ■ Different ways information is conveyed, no Standard Operating Procedures ■ Consistently revising documents but not implementing © Lean Healthcare Consultants Pty Ltd Define52 Example WORKTIME Table Waste Examples Time or inventory lost per year Benefits vs. Effort Benefits V human effort if the waste was eliminated Waiting Patients wait 30 mins for transport to wards (50pts x 356 days) Pts wait 7.5 weeks per year Pts are admitted and receive treatment sooner Overproduction Staff spend 10mins per day looking for e files as naming convention not followed (20 staff x 10 mins) 20.5 working weeks Staff process work quicker and meet deadlines Reduce frustration and reduce rework Rejects Knowledge OT’s spend 150 minutes per week collecting loaned equipment from patients at their homes to reissue it to new pts (3 OT’s) 9.7 weeks of Clinical OT time More Clinical time available to complete clinical assessments Transport Inventory Motion Doctors walk Xray requests to Radiology which takes 7 mins x 4 times a day 4.1 weeks doctor time per year Dr available to do more clinical care Execution Define WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 53 Collecting Voice of the Staff – Example Survey Why use it? Staff feedback provides an important perspective and insight into the quality of the patient pathway. Who does it? The project team. What does it do? Provides feedback from staff members regarding their perspectives of the quality of the patient journey. How do you do it? The staff you interview may work in various teams across the patient pathway, rather than within one particular team. Identify a key contact and ask them for insights into who to interview (in other words, those staff best able to give an informed view of different aspects of the service), and to provide introductions, in order to ‘open doors’. Make sure the interviewees are drawn from roles across the entire patient pathway, giving a diversity of views and roles. Once you have selected people, you may need to contact potential participants several times to finalise appointments. Informal, impromptu interviews can be useful for catching key people who are particularly busy. Also refer to the Voice of the Patient Toolkit. The CTQ requirements are referred to throughout the entire CSR process to guide: ■ prioritisation of what is measured ■ prioritisation of what is analysed ■ solution generation ■ prioritisation for solution implementation It ensures the CSR process is patient centred, and allows the input of staff and the organisation. Who does it? The project team members involved with collecting the Voice of Staff, Patient and Organisation. What does it do? Helps teams to define what the boundaries of acceptable service/ product/ cost for key patient/ staff issues are, and provides the focus for all solutions. How do you do it? 1. Collate the following information and theme into: ■ Voice of the staff – What is important to the staff? ■ Voice of the patient – What is important to the patient? ■ Voice of the organisation – What is important to the hospital? Define WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 57Example Critical to Quality spider web Define58 Define WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 59 Measure Measure is the second stage in the DMAIC process. The purpose of this stage is to validate the information collected in Define, using data to confirm the existence and extent of problems: ■ Staff have said that a particular step in the process causes the biggest delays, but if we measure the time taken, does it verify or disprove this claim? How does it compare to other steps? To the same process elsewhere? ■ Patients have told us where they have had good and bad experiences, but is this representative? Has it improved over time? ■ Early baseline or high level data can point us to where or when a problem might exist, and with further data mining we can reveal some gems. Key steps include translating issues and delays into measures, creating a data collection plan, collecting data to assess the size and impact and identifying current performance baselines. The outputs of Define (process map, issues list, SIPOC etc) therefore become the inputs of Measure. Make contact with the HSIU analyst team or on site analyst early on, before you know what data you want. Key Activities: ■ Identify potential measures ■ Develop a Data Measurement Plan (DMP) ■ Define clear data definitions ■ Determine whether data is available, or will need to be collected ■ Develop data collection tools ■ Gather data, Evaluate data and communicate to stakeholders Overview of Key Activities Step 1: Identify Potential Measures Translating Delays and Issues into Measure Define has two outputs in particular which help to develop data measures – process maps and issues lists. Measure60 Figure 6: Data measures – Process map with issues list From Delays to Measures: The process map includes points of delay and shows where in the process issues are clustered. ■ Every delay impacts on the total time between two points in the process you have mapped ■ Firstly measure the smallest time that is available electronically ■ Determine if there is sufficient evidence of a delay affecting a large enough group of patients to proceed further ■ Determine if manual data is already being collected for more precise time points and if so, conduct a manual audit to collect a sample of the data ■ If no other data is available do a manual data collection of the two time points required to closely measure the delay Measure WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 61 From Issues to Measures Figure 7: Issues list The list of issues collected in Define indicates what may be causing delays or having other impacts on the process: ■ Every issue has some impact on the process but not all issues can be measured ■ Review issues to determine if they are measurable – what effect do they have and is this measurable? ■ Note where issues are clustered in the process and if there are issues that naturally group together ■ If measurable, look for electronic data which is already available – Look for effect at the electronically measurable level – Decide if further information is required – Collect manual data if required Is it measurable? Every issue may have some impact on the process, but not all issues can (or should) be measured. Ask yourself, “is this issue quantifiable?” That is, can you answer a question about the issue with a number: how long did it take? How many people or items did it involve? How much did it cost? Assessing issues for measurability in this way can help to exclude problems that are too vague, too high level or involve too many elements. Issues that cannot be measured in this way should not necessarily be excluded but may require qualitative data to analyse. Develop an initial log of potential measures by reviewing issues/delays from the process map and consider if and how they are measureable. Step 2: Develop a Data Measurement Plan A Data Measurement Plan details everything you intend to measure, and is used to ascertain whether it is indeed a significant issue. Create a Data Measurement Plan When creating your plan consider the following: ■ Whether you are gathering data on legitimate areas of concern or areas with potential opportunity gains ■ Ensure the issues that are measured relate to what has been determined in the critical to quality requirements. ■ The availability of the data ■ The potential accuracy and completeness of the data ■ The complexity of the data ■ Whether the data can be benchmarked against other organisations ■ If the data will be a helpful ongoing measure of performance ■ If manual data collection is required, the time and resources required to collect each element of the data. You may need to prioritise which measures to invest time in collecting It is useful to liaise with a data analyst before you start your data collection to gain expert advice on your data definitions and how best to gather information. Step 3: Define clear data definitions Develop operational definition within the data management plan An operational definition has two components 1. A clear description of the specific attribute of the problem – What is to be measured 2. The steps to get the value for the attribute – What cases are included and excluded (scope) – How it is calculated (consistency) – What is the source of the data (reliability) Measure WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 63 Data definitions are critical to ensuring that the data collected reflects the requirements of the reform process, and that there is a common understanding of what is being talked about. For example, if I ask you about the rules of “football” am I talking about AFL, NRL, NFL or soccer? See the end of this section for an example of a data management plan, including an example of an operational definition of an issue. Step 4: Determine whether data is available, or will need to be collected Discuss your data requirements initially with a data analyst. There is a range of data regularly being captured by hospitals and the Department. If there is already data that relates to your project, this could: ■ Help clarify the scope of the issue ■ Help determine the size of the problem - if the issue or delay affects a large enough group of patients to proceed ■ Help guide you to where the problem is ■ Save you collecting data manually ■ Confirm what (if any) manual data needs to be collected Existing data includes (but is not limited to): ■ EDIS – Emergency data ■ HCare – Emergency and Inpatient data for WACHS ■ TOPAS – Inpatient data Events and Event History ■ TOPAS – Outpatient data Referrals and Appointments ■ RIS – Radiology times request received to report available ■ TMS – Theatre data ■ WebPAS – Replaces TOPAS and HCare Wherever possible, try to use an existing data collection method in the first instance rather than creating your own. Refer back to step 2 of Project Initiation for a list of examples of existing data sources which you can utilise to collect your data. If no other data is available a manual data collection can be completed. Step 5: Develop data collection tools How do you develop data collection tools? ■ Where available collect data from existing paperwork, such as notes, forms and log books ■ If not available collect the data in real time ■ Where available use proven collection tools ■ Be clear on the reason for collection- do not collect data ‘just in case’ ■ Provide clear instructions to collectors ■ Don’t assume anything, a common understanding of any measures, terms, etc are required ■ Keep it as simple as possible ■ If time permits test the tool first Measure64 ■ If possible, collect data into a spreadsheet to avoid batch entry (see tips on creating spreadsheets and entering data) ■ Be aware of the Hawthorne effect – collecting data can have an impact on performance of those being observed. If measuring delays, collect the smallest possible time points (available electronically). This will allow you to still use the data even if your larger measures change as the smaller time points can be summed together to make the measure. For example instead of initially measuring time from theatres to the ward, instead collect from theatres to recovery, recovery to ward, which can be summed to make the measure from theatres to ward. Measures may also involve a quantity other than time. For example: the number of patients waiting for a procedure; the number, or proportion of times a form is used (or not used); the frequency of adverse events; or the number and type of cancellations. Again, liaise with your data analysts to help make the data meaningful. Measure66 Step 8: Qualitative data What is qualitative data? ■ Any information that is not numerical ■ Usually verbal responses, and is usually used to frame the Voices of the patient and staff ■ It shows what can happen rather than what usually happens ■ Helps show the “what”, “why” and “how” rather than the “is” or “does” ■ Sources may include existing complaints registers, satisfaction surveys ■ If the data you need is not available, you can use interviews, focus groups, open ended surveys and observation ■ As with other data collection you should be clear and specific about what you want to know ■ Analysis is more time consuming as you need to translate a large amount of varied information into useable data ■ Invaluable in gathering ‘stories’ or case studies to illustrate a problem ■ Can have a much greater emotional impact (and therefore motivate more) than data ■ Discuss qualitative data collection and analysis with analysts before proceeding Information is also available in the Voice of the Patient Toolkit, regarding conducting qualitative data analysis: ■ Use process maps and issues list to identify possible measures ■ Use a data analyst throughout Measure to ensure you get the most appropriate and correct information from the process ■ Use data to determine if there is problem, where the problem is, and how big it is ■ Ensure the data is communicated to all applicable stakeholders; a variety of methods may need to be used – refer to your communications log.
