Analysis begins to examine the cause and effect relationship between factors (e.g. does x impact y?). This section outlines methods used to identify, group and rate issues to discover the underlying ‘root causes’ of a problem. The potential root cause is then validated using hypothesis testing to determine if it is having the predicted impact. The Analyse phase aims to differentiate between ‘popular’ issues, symptomatic causes and the underlying causes. What you or others think is the primary cause may not be impacting the problem significantly or on a large scale. Analyse does not involve solution generation; any solutions raised during this phase should be noted and “car parked” for the Improve phase. Key Activities: ■ Run analyse sessions with key stakeholders ■ Identify and validate the root cause(s) ■ Communicate the findings Overview of Key Activities Step 1: Run Analyse Sessions Analyse sessions are a group technique used to capture stakeholders’ ideas of potential root causes. Different groups of stakeholders may have different views of from where the problem originates, so it is beneficial to involve a variety of stakeholders which represent the different areas covered in the project. Analyse sessions involve examining a specific problem statement and listing possible causes of a problem. The session may also be used to explore the relationships that exist between the causes to help differentiate between symptomatic causes and underlying causes. The session may also allow the stated causes to be ranked by the participants on what they think is the most important or impactful causes. The underlying (or root) cause is the first, causal factor in the chain that ultimately is responsible for causing the undesired outcome. The symptomatic cause can sometimes be considered to bring about the issue but, while it may be contributing to the issue, it is really just one of the links in the chain that has come about because of the underlying/root cause. It is important to be aware that the purpose of an Analyse session is to identify deficiencies or gaps in a process and to not jump to solutions. Commonly suggested “causes” such as a lack of beds, staff or theatres are often raised as potential solutions but do not assist in uncovering the true causes. Consideration should also be given to framing the problem statement in a factual, data driven way. The statement is often posed as a question and should be devoid of myths, assumptions, judgement and blame. Analyse72 Diagnosing and Rating the Causes There are a number of tools and techniques used to capture causes. Each method is used for a different purpose, situation, group setting or facilitator preference. Tools used to identify, examine and rank causes include: ■ Cause and Effect Diagram (Fishbone/Ishikawa) ■ Affinity Map ■ Five Why’s ■ Why-Because Diagram ■ Inter-relationship Diagraph ■ Nominal Grouping Technique Different tools have different purposes, as summarised below. Table 3: Tool purposes Generating issues Grouping issues Rating issues Cause and effect 4 4 Affinity diagram 4 4 4 5 whys 4 Why/because 4 4 Interrelationship diagraph 4 Nominal Group Technique 4 See the end of this section for examples of these tools. Project teams may consider running several Analyse sessions, as each session usually only focuses on one problem statement and some projects may have multiple statements that need to be explored. The outcome of an Analyse session is to have generated potential root causes that will be validated. Additional considerations for conducting an Analyse session: ■ Be creative and innovative, without judgement or premature evaluation ■ Seek input from a wide range of stakeholders – as with previous group sessions, this will involve extensive communication with stakeholders, through a number of channels to maximise attendance ■ Be specific and drill down on issues to get to the root cause ■ Avoid jumping to solutions, however note them if they arise ■ Display data collected, process maps and the project’s CTQ requirements Step 2: Identify and validate the Root Cause(s) Once potential root cause(s) have been identified, the next step is to validate them using hypothesis testing. An hypothesis is a statement about the relationship between two or more factors; a proposed explanation as to why something is occurring. An hypothesis statement is written to validate the identified root cause’s effect on the stated problem. The project team may want to work with a data analyst to develop and test the hypothesis. The process used for hypothesis testing involves: Analyse WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 73 ■ Stating the hypothesis (null and alternative) ■ Collecting data ■ Conducting statistical tests to prove (or disprove) the hypothesis The statistical tests used will depend on the type of data collected (including T-Test, ANOVA and Chi-Squared test). It should be noted that even if the relationship between two factors is statistically significant it does not necessarily mean the relationship is important or wide spread. The purpose of validating root causes is to provide factual evidence of a cause’s impact and to ensure time and resources are appropriately directed to improving areas that are significantly affecting the problem or process. You may find you have groups of people who believe or do not believe something is a causal factor – having a root cause validated (or disproved) is a powerful piece of evidence to help change their thinking. See the end of this section for more on validating the root cause. Step 3: Communicate the Findings After the root causes have been validated, stakeholders need to be informed of the findings. There are a number of ways the findings can be communicated, depending on the resources available, the size of the project and the stakeholders involved. Data Fair A visual event used to share the Analyse process and findings through posters and storyboards. It is an opportunity to display the outcomes and achievements from each section of the DMA process including process maps, Voice of the Patient, data, and the validated root causes. Key benefits of the data fair are the opportunity for stakeholder interaction and the chance to raise the awareness of the project amongst staff, including those who may not be directly involved. A limitation of the data fair is the time and resources involved including a venue, display materials, project team availability and advertising. Data fairs are recommended for large scale projects. Consult your CSR Advisor at the HSIU for assistance in identifying if your project would suit a data fair, and how to go about running one. Presentation(s) Validated root causes may also be communicated to stakeholders during a presentation, either in a stand-alone session or part of a pre-existing meeting or training. This method generally requires less resources and time compared to a data fair, whilst still allowing a chance for the project team to raise the awareness of the project and explain the process and findings that have occurred to date. Other Methods Other methods for communicating findings back to stakeholders include: ■ A display on wards, notice boards or in common areas. This method does not allow for interaction and ability to answer questions about the process or findings ■ Written communication via email or newsletter. Often the simplest method, written communication does not allow interaction with stakeholders or a chance to explain the process or data. There may also be difficulties in ensuring the stakeholders take the time to read the findings ■ Using a combination of the methods listed above to ensure the maximum audience are communicated with and informed Analyse74 ■ Identifying causes in the Analyse phase involves continually asking WHY. Broad and lateral thinking will help identify causes. Causes should be based on factual information and not just assumptions ■ Do not avoid contentious causes ■ Carefully select the analysis tool used, considering group size, power dynamic and frame problem statement appropriately Before moving on to Improve, have you… At the end of Analysis you will have: 4	Identified root cause(s) 4	Validated root cause(s) 4	Communicated findings with stakeholders? Make use of a data analyst for hypothesis testing if the team has no prior experience. Analyse sessions involve examining a specific problem statement and listing possible causes of a problem. The session may also be used to explore the relationships that exist between the causes to help differentiate between symptomatic causes and underlying causes. The session may also allow the stated causes to be ranked by the participants on what they think is the most important or impactful causes. ‘Y’ Why? Because Why? Because Why? Because ‘X’ The underlying (or root) cause is the first causal factor in the chain that ultimately is responsible for causing the undesired outcome. The symptomatic cause can sometimes be considered to bring about the issue but, while it may be contributing to the issue, it is really just one of the links in the chain that has come about because of the underlying/root cause. It is important to be aware that the purpose of an Analyse session is to identify deficiencies or gaps in a process and to not jump to solutions. Commonly suggested “causes” such as a lack of beds, staff or theatres are often raised as potential solutions but do not assist in uncovering the true causes. Consideration should also be given to framing the problem statement in a factual, data driven way. The statement is often posed as a question and should be devoid of myths, assumptions, judgement and blame. Why use it? To examine possible causes of a specific problem, to explore the relationships between causes to help identify underlying causes, and to identify what the participants of the session think are the most important or impactful causes. Analyse76 Who does it? The project team organises and facilitates the session with the staff involved with the area being redesigned, and preferably those who also attended the process mapping sessions. What does it do? Identifies deficiencies or gaps in a process. The root cause analysis should provide a ‘waterfall’ of information that starts with the question (Y) and ends with the root cause (X). The root cause is the reason at the bottom of the waterfall. How do you do it? A root cause analysis session needs: ■ A clear problem statement ■ A leader ■ Stakeholders The most important guideline for the session itself is that the purpose is to learn and to improve, not to assign blame or to vent. Getting Going ■ Be very clear on the data story - make sure all the data is related and leads to the question the session has been set up to solve. ■ Before the session, SIPOC the process that the question is related to. This can really help ensure you invite the right people. ■ At the session, make sure the start and end of the process in hand is very clear to everyone in the room ■ There are various ways to set the session up, the silent affinity mapping is an example of a tool that works really well to gather information from a group. When doing this, do encourage all participants to get involved at the board - this ensures that everyone has read all the post-it notes and saves time in the RCA exercise. ■ Spend a bit of time processing the information from the affinity mapping -especially to make sure that each cluster of notes really are separate issues (they can be variations on a theme i.e. ‘waiting for test results’ may actually be part of ‘delays in decision making’ rather than separate to it). Reflect back to the group your conclusion on the real issue being explored in each cluster and use that as your starting point for the RCA. Deciding which issue to explore: The group may raise a large number of issues. As a group it is important to identify which are considered the most important to progress on with. Rank the issues, using nominal grouping technique or Ranking and Rating (each person assigning sticky dots for the issues they feel are their highest priorities). The issue ranked the highest (with the most number of dots) is analysed. Techniques for Analysis The heart of the meeting is the analysis itself. For each problem, we want to ask “why did that happen?” and “why wasn’t it prevented by our process?” See the end of this section for a range of root cause analysis tools. Analyse WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 77 Root Cause Analysis Tips The most challenging part is the Root Cause Analysis (RCA) exercise itself. There are a few potential pitfalls: E.g.: ‘The problem is we don’t have enough staff/beds/PCAas/etc etc’. This often comes up. The follow up why is not ‘why don’t we have enough ....’ but rather ‘why don’t you have a nurse/bed/pca etc when you need one?’ or ‘why don’t we have the bed the patient needs when they need it’. E.g.: ‘The nurses/PCAs/doctors etc are too busy’. Rather than following up with ‘why are they so busy’ you need to get to ‘why are they not available to do what the patient needs when the patient needs it’ or possibly ‘why does that need to be done or why do you need them to do that? (this could help elicit issues which have been flagged as duplication or being done by the wrong role). RCA’s tend to focus on delays - don’t forget about variation too –’is it always done like that?’ If not, ask ‘why isn’t it always done in the same way?’ to understand the root causes of variation. Check the size of the issue as you go along - avoid spending time on those which affect very few patients Other tips A nominated solutions note-taker in the room is helpful, to ensure the solution is captured but not given too much attention while the focus is on delving into problem causes. Cause and effect diagram (Fishbone diagram/Ishikawa ) Why use it? It provides a systematic way of looking at effects and the causes that create or contribute to those effects. They are useful for: ■ Studying a problem/ issue to determine the root cause ■ Studying all the possible reasons why a process is beginning to have difficulties, problems or breakdowns ■ Identifying areas for data collection ■ Studying why a process is not performing properly or producing the desired results ■ Encouraging group participation ■ Increasing process knowledge ■ Identifying areas for collecting data Who does it? The project team, with key staff whose work relates to the analyse question. Analyse78 What does it do? This diagram is a tool that: ■ Helps organise brainstorming ideas and identifying root causes of a problem ■ Helps visualise the relationship between a problem and possible underlying causes ■ Provides a common understanding of the problem and exposes the potential causes of the problem How do you do it? 1. Draw the diagram and list the problem/ issue to be studied in the “head of the fish”. 2. Label each skeletal bone of the fish: Identify and choose the categories of causes and label each branch of the fishbone. There are traditional categories that are used when labelling each skeletal bone, these are typically 4 P’s: a. Place b. Procedure c. People d. Policies Other categories, in addition to the 4Ps, can be assigned if necessary. 3. For each cause, ask ‘Why does this happen?”, and attach information as another bone of the category branch. Ask questions here to generate ideas in order to seek potential root causes. Use an idea generating technique (eg. brainstorming) to identify the factors within each category that may be affecting the problem/ issue and/ or effect being studied (head of fish). The team should ask, “What are the issues affecting/ causing each category?” Repeat this procedure with each factor under the category to produce sub-factors. Continue asking, “Why is this happening?” and put additional segments under each factor and subsequently under each sub-factor. Continue until no more useful information is provided. 4. Analyse the results of the fishbone after each team member agrees that an adequate amount of detail has been provided under each major category. Do this by looking for those items that appear in more than one category. These become the ‘most likely causes’. Analyse WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 79 For those items identified as the ‘most likely causes’, the team should reach consensus on listing those items in priority order with the first item being the most probable cause. 5. The most likely causes will then be used to develop hypotheses regarding the true root cause for the issue. Example of cause and effect diagram Example Statement: Why does it take 3 hours 48 mins for triage 4 and 5 patients to be seen by a doctor? Effect: The hospital does not meet the performance indicator for time seen in the appropriate time for this triage category (60 minutes and 120 minutes) People Facilities/Design ■ Triage nurse staffing levels (in the winter there should be 2 triage nurses plus the CIN) ■ Mix of staff – junior registrars seeing new patients can delays triage times ■ Triage nurses feel uncomfortable educating patients at the point of triage and sending them home/ refer to other services ■ Lack of physical space to fast track low acuity patients ■ Waitin area: ■ No clear delineation between triage groups in the waiting area ie coloured chairs (red triage que seats are on the way) ■ Waiting area in triage inadequate and exposed. Poor layout ■ Lack of access to computers can delay the “time seen” being entered into healthcare ■ Lack of ownership of KPIs – who is capturing the reasons for delays, potential ways to improve ■ Patients that depart prior to being seen by Medical Officer may not be removed from system ■ Medical education times (8am) prevent new patients being seen - No protected time for conflict between work and education, e.g. could get consultants to see new patients during these times ■ Delays in Medical Officers seeing new patients - busy managing other patients and seeing multiple T3 patients ■ Triage nurse – interruptions can cause back-up of new patients in cue, handovers can cause delays Systems/Monitoring Processes Analyse80 Affinity Diagram Why use it? It allows a team to creatively generate and “weight” a large number of issues. It is a good tool to use if there is a strong personality in the group or a disparity in staff members hierarchy/status as this technique allows every stakeholder to add ideas without feeling threatened. Who does it? The project team, with key staff whose work relates to the analyse question. What does it do? Brainstorms various issues related to an area of a process map; then organises and summarises groupings or common themes of these issues as a way to help understand the essence of the problem. How do you do it? 1. Generate ideas – Individuals brainstorm as many ideas or issues as possible, without talking to other people – Record one issue per Post-it note 2. Display ideas – Stick post-it notes on a wall or table in a random manner 3. Sort ideas into groups – Without talking, sort issues simultaneously into related groupings. Each team member moves the post-it notes to where they think it fits best. – Sorting will slow down or stop when each person feels sufficiently comfortable with the groupings. It is OK for some notes to stand alone. 4. Create header cards – For each grouping, assign a word or phase that describes the central theme as the header. 5. The team assigns sticky dots to each grouped cause to ascertain which is the most important problem statement. Analyse82 Five Whys Why use it? It provides a systematic questioning technique to challenge assumptions, is used to cross validate the potential causes of an issue/ problem being studied. Who does it? The project team, with key staff whose work relates to the analyse question. What does it do? It can help you to arrive at the potential root cause of the problem by progressively becoming more and more difficult and thought provoking as you get deeper and deeper into the root causes and determining the relationship between different root causes of a problem. How do you do it? The facilitator asks others ‘why is this happening?’ five times to assist in drilling down to the potential root cause. Start by grouping a number of common issues into one problem statement and writing this at the beginning of the diagram. As more ‘Why’ questions are asked, the causes become progressively more difficult and more thought provoking. There also might be situations where more than 5 why questions will need to be asked. When these situations occur simply ask others to provide more information. This can be achieved by asking questions, such as: ■ Could you please explain that? ■ What’s the next level? ■ Why is this happening? This tool can be useful when used in conjunction with the fish bone diagram – as the ‘why?’ questioning technique can be used for each issue allocated to a bone of the fish. Analyse WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 83Example Five WhysWhy did this happen?→ Patient is delayed getting to theatre.Why?→ It takes too long to admit patient?Why?→ Staff are busy chasing up notes.Why?→ Notes are elsewhere.Why?→ Notes have not been delivered.Potential root cause→ There isn’t a process for delivery Analyse84 Why-Because Diagram Why use it? It provides a systematic questioning technique to challenge assumptions, is used to cross validate the potential causes of an issue/ problem being studied. Who does it? The project team, with key staff whose work relates to the analyse question. What does it do? This technique, while similar to the ‘Five Whys’ tool above, allows you to drill down to the root cause of a problem while avoiding the limitation of using just five why questions. Also, if a problem is multifactorial, this technique allows you to explore all factors. This technique is also best in the case that there are many initial ‘why’ questions. How do you do it? ■ Write the problem you want to analyse in the left box ■ Ask “why” the problem occurs ■ Note the answer(s) in a connecting box ■ Repeat the previous two steps until a particular path is exhausted ■ Continue for all boxes until the exercise is completed ■ Group the list of causes by relationships or common factors Analyse WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 85AnalyseBecause...Because...Why?Because...Problem StatementWhy?Because...Because...Because...Why?Because...Why-because template 86 Interrelationship Diagraph Why use it? When you have several issues which are related, and you would like to understand which issues are more causal and which are more symptomatic. This tool should identify the main cause of all the other issues. Who does it? The project team, with key staff whose work relates to the analyse question. What does it do? Explores the cause and effect relationships among all the issues, including the most controversial. How do you do it? 1. The interrelationship diagraph is used following on from the completion of another analyse tool such as a why-because or affinity mapping, in the same session. 2. Use the main potential root causes identified from the previous analyse activity. 3. Choose an issue and work through all the other ideas in sequence 4. Look for cause/influence relationships between all of the ideas and draw relationship arrows. Ask the question does ‘A’ have more influence on ‘B’, or does ‘B’ have more influence on ‘A’? Ask this question for all issues. 5. An outgoing arrow from the idea indicates that it is the stronger influence. Do not draw two-headed arrows. If the group does not feel there is a relationship or influence between two areas do not draw an arrow. 6. Tally the number of outgoing (O) and incoming (I) arrows and select key items for further analysis. The item(s) with the highest number of outgoing arrows indicates an item that is a root cause or driver Analyse WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 87 Interrelationship diagram template A B C DE F Analyse88 Nominal Grouping Technique Why use it? This technique allows a team to quickly create a list of options and rank them according to which are the most important. It neutralises the domination of the loudest person, or the person with the most authority, over the decision making process. This is also a good tool to use when dealing with controversial or emotional issues, or when a group is stuck. Who does it? The project team, with participants of an analyse session. What does it do? Enables a group to generate and prioritise a large number of issues within a structure, while allowing everyone to have an equal voice. This tool can also help a team achieve consensus about the relative importance of issues- the final result may not be everyone’s first priority, but they can live with it. It is useful when you need to: ■ Reduce the number of issues for easier handling ■ Lessen the influence of a dominant participant ■ Get input from all team members ■ Rank items in priority order How do you do it? 1. Ensure the group understands the analyse question that you are trying to answer. 2. Generate the list of potential root causes to be prioritised (Affinity mapping is a good way to do this). 3. Eliminate any duplication and/or clarify meanings of any statements 4. Assign a letter to each statement or grouping. 5. Each team member records the corresponding letters on a piece of paper and assigns a rank order to the statements (silently and without discussion) 6. Use the highest number as the most important statement 7. Tally the rankings of all team members 8. Alternatively statements can be weighted by team members assigning 100 points across all statements 9. The ranking and rating does not have to be done in a group – a survey could also be used. Analyse WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 89Example: Nominal grouping technique templateTeam member#1 #2 #3#4 #5TotalStatementsA45221=14B54535=22C31344=15D22152=11E13413=13 Analyse90 Hypothesis testing Why use it? Hypothesis testing examines whether the root cause that you have identified is in fact a significant cause of issues or delays in the system. Who does it? Hypothesis testing is completed by the data analyst. To test a hypothesis the analyst will first translate the hypothesis into a test using existing or collectable data. What does it do? The test may examine: 1. The difference between groups of data. 2. If an increase in one value has a related increase (or decrease) in another value. 3. If the presence (or not) of one item predicts the presence (or not) of another. Different techniques are used to test these different types of scenarios. How do you do it? The project teams will be required to take their root cause analysis to their data analyst. They will then need to collect new data specific to the identified hypothesis, so that the data analyst will be able to analyse the data and indicate whether the identified root cause is indeed the source of the problems identified in the process. Analyse WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 91 Improve The Improve phase of DMAIC primarily focuses on solution design and preliminary construction. Improve involves generating solutions to address validated root causes raised in Analyse, while ensuring the potential solutions are in keeping with the project aims and the Critical to Quality requirements. During Improve it is important to be creative while generating ideas, be willing to move from minor modifications to substantial change, and then move on to practical planning of selected solutions. Key Activities: ■ Vision development ■ Solution generation ■ Evaluate and prioritise solutions ■ Solution sign-off ■ Communicate the solutions Overview of Key Activities Step 1: Vision Development With the root causes of a problem identified and validated, the next step in the process is to generate solution ideas which will address the causes and make improvements. Developing a Vision The first step in developing solutions is to set an agreed vision for the project. A vision is a simple statement which indicates the desired state - the outcomes that the project wishes to achieve, including meeting the Critical to Quality requirements. This is done with the project team; who should be instructed to imagine that the problem no longer existed and was turned around completely. Ask yourself: ■ What would we see? ■ What would the patients receive in terms of service? ■ How would the staff feel? ■ What would the hospital and patients gain? Use the answers to these questions to form your vision statement. The vision should not prescribe the way to get there, for example, “we will have Criteria Led Discharge in all medical wards”, it is the desired end of state, for example, “discharge process will be transparent so that all staff and patients know at all times when the patient is expected to leave the hospital and/or what they are waiting for. All staff will understand their role with respect to discharge processes”. The purpose of creating a common vision is to help ensure the suggested solutions are aimed at achieving the desired outcomes. Solutions should address how we get from the current state to the desired state. Improve92 Step 2: Solution Generation With the vision set, the next step is brainstorming ideas and concepts that will help the project reach its desired state. This process usually involves a group session with stakeholders to generate as many solutions as possible that will address the problem or cause. The purpose of holding a group solution session is to allow an opportunity to build on various stakeholders’ ideas to create innovative solutions whilst involving those who will be most likely affected by the change. Engaging stakeholders during this phase will help them to understand the logic behind the solutions that were suggested. It also serves to give stakeholders a sense of ownership of the solutions which will be beneficial in the change management component of the project. Ensure during the group solution generation session that the Vision statement and Critical to Quality requirements are clearly displayed in the room to help remind participants of the desired state and outcomes. See the end of this section for more on how to conduct an Ideas Generation session. Step 3: Evaluate and Prioritise Solutions Solution Assessment The purpose of solution assessment is to determine the solutions that will address the problem and have the most benefit for staff and patients, whilst considering resourcing and budget constraints. As it is often not feasible to implement all solutions suggested, project teams should consider methods to prioritise or select the most appropriate solutions. Assessing the highest ranking solutions from the generation session is a good place to start. It is important to consider assessing solutions against criteria such as: ■ Which solution/s is most likely to address the problem or root cause ■ Which solution will have the most benefit to staff and patients ■ Which are achievable within budget and resources ■ What are the timeframes likely to be for achievement and does this fit within the project timeframes A solutions assessment matrix can be used as a method to rank solutions. The matrix may be completed by the project team, stakeholder and/or executive. The assessment matrix involves rating each solution against a set of criteria and assigning a score, helping to prioritise or select solutions. Using a documented assessment matrix can also be useful in ensuring consistency in thought across the project team members, making decisions transparent, and enabling easier communication to stakeholders as to why certain solutions have or have not been progressed. The table below is an example of how to rank your solutions in priority, in terms of patient benefit, sustainability, complexity of their implementation, impact on service provision and reform alignment. Improve WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 93 Table 4: Potential criteria for assessment matrix Each element should be assigned a ranking score with 5 being the highest score. Criteria Descriptor (elements to think about when weighting) 1 Patient benefit 5= Most Benefit; 0 Least benefit ■ Patients requirements (timeliness, communication and information, safety, health outcomes) 2 Sustainability of outcomes 5 = Will be sustained; 0 = Not sustainable ■ Positive outcomes will occur (improved performance, increased efficiencies) ■ Outcomes can be maintained (workforce and funding are secured in future recurrent funding) ■ Ownership and accountability (the project aims will become core business) 3 Complexity of implementation 5 = feasible; 0 = Not feasible now ■ Barriers to implementation (staffing, funding, stakeholder acceptance) ■ Change required to implement (few, some, considerable, major) ■ Readiness for change, staff acceptance 4 Potential impact of service provision 5 = Dependencies accounted for; 0 = Too dependent on parallel system level projects ■ Impact on related projects or processes ■ Interdependencies with other projects (ICT dependent on Roll out WebPAS ■ Potential impact on goal statement, milestones and objectives 5 Strategic/reform alignment 5 = No conflicts; 0 = Does not align with government policy ■ Executive stakeholder acceptance ■ Project aligns with parallel reform projects occurring as part of NEAT and NEST reform ■ WA Health and HS policies, strategies and reform priorities (ie CSF) 6 Project specific requirements 5 = Meet all CTQ: 0=Meets some CTQ ■ Project’s Critical to Quality requirements ■ Project’s vision At this step it is also beneficial to consider the inter-dependencies between prospective solutions and how this will impact selection and implementation. Some solutions may be collaborative; it may be easy and logical to implement them in tandem, or they may share similar resources or change requirements. Alternatively, there also may be solutions that are mutually exclusive; not inter-related or similar to any other solutions and that will need to be implemented individually. When evaluating solutions, thought should be given to relevant guidelines, legislation and agreements, as these will impact upon the priority for implementation of the solutions. Improve94 Outlining the desired state Once you have brainstormed and ranked solutions to the issues identified in the process map, these can be translated these into a ‘desired’ process map – how it would ideally look if all the solutions were able to be implemented, and CTQ requirements considered. This is conducted in a group session with all your stakeholders by using the Greenfield mapping tool. This tool can also be used as an idea generation method, allowing people to mentally ‘walk through’ the desired end state and raising ideas for change at each step See the end of this section for instruction on how to use Greenfield mapping. Step 4: Solution Sign-off Following the selection and prioritisation of solutions, project teams need approval to progress solutions from the ideas stage into reality. In order to do this a project charter needs to be created for each of the proposed solutions. The project charter allows high- level planning of the solution and documenting key information that will allow the idea to move from a concept into implementation. At this stage, the charter is being written for the executive sponsor or team, those who are able to make a decision about progressing solutions, and approving costs or resources associated with the solutions. At this stage it is important to put in as much effort as required in order to present a comprehensive case to the executive, without spending too much time on the specifics – if the solution is not endorsed you do not want to have spent too much time writing up comprehensive stakeholder and risk management plans. If you are putting up multiple solutions for approval, make any interrelationship between the solutions clear. Step 5: Communicate the Solution As with the data presentation in Analyse (see step 3 of Analyse), communicating solutions developed and planned is a necessary step to make staff aware of the imminent change and the reasoning behind it. Methods similar to those detailed under data presentation can be used, such as holding a solution fair, presenting at meetings, displaying information on walls, circulating written information via email or newsletter or using a combination of these. The pros and cons detailed in Analyse are also relevant for solution presentation. The type of presentation will depend on the size of the changes, the facility and the stakeholders. As with other stakeholder events, as many people as possible should be communicated with to raise awareness. Ensure key stakeholders are: ■ Aware of what changes will be occurring ■ What the changes will involve for them ■ When they will be occurring ■ Who to contact Improve WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 95 ■ Work with stakeholders to develop as many creative and innovative solutions as possible ■ Always consider the project’s CTQ requirements when prioritising solutions. Achieving these requirements will increase the likelihood of successful implementation ■ Meticulously planning and preparing for solution implementation will increase the chance of successful change Before moving on to Control, have you… 4	Developed a vision of the desired state? 4	Conducted an ideas generation session? 4	Prioritised solutions? 4	Achieved sign-off for solutions to progress? 4	Communicated the planned solutions to stakeholders? Hints and Tips Improve96 Tools and Resources Conducting an Ideas Generation Session Why use it? To identify the solutions that will change the process to resemble the vision statement. Who does it? The project team, with all stakeholders who will be affected by changes to the process. It is important to not leave anyone out as the ideas generation session is an opportunity to give these stakeholders a sense of ownership over the changes, hence minimising potential opposition to change. It is beneficial to involve a variety of stakeholders which represent the different areas covered in the project. What does it do? Ideas generation sessions are a group technique used to capture stakeholders’ ideas on potential solutions. Different groups of stakeholders may have different views on what the best solution is. How do you do it? Have the root causes that were identified in the Analyse Session and the vision statement on display. The most important guideline for the session itself is that the purpose is to learn and to improve, not to assign blame or to vent. Getting Going ■ Be very clear on the root causes - make sure you have all the data which validates the root causes to the problems. ■ There are various ways to set the session up - the silent affinity mapping is an example of a tool that works well to gather information from a group. When doing this, do encourage all participants to get involved at the board - this ensures that everyone has read all the post-it notes Affinity mapping is a tool that is often used in solution generation. Affinity mapping: 1. Generate ideas Individuals brainstorm as many ideas or solutions as possible without talking to other people Record each solution on a Post-it note 2. Display ideas Stick post-it notes on a wall or table in a random manner Improve WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 97 3. Sort ideas into groups Without talking, sort issues into related groupings. Each team member moves the post- it notes to where they think it fits best. Sorting will slow down or stop when each person feels sufficiently comfortable with the groupings. It is OK for some notes to stand alone. 4. Create header cards For each grouping, assign a word or phase that describes the central theme as the header. Refer back to the tools in ‘Analyse’ for more about affinity mapping. Spend a bit of time processing the information from the affinity mapping -especially to make sure that each cluster of notes really are separate solutions. Reflect back to the group your conclusion on the main solutions and use that as your starting point for finalising the solutions. Deciding which solution to explore: Depending on the group, you could use nominal grouping to rank the solutions (eg: five dots for the priority 1 solution, three for the second and one for the third). The solution ranked the highest (with the most number of dots) is furthered. Effective Ideas Generation DO DON’T ■ Think outside the square ■ Challenge the existing status quo ■ Understand the problem prior to beginning the session ■ Allow individuals to complete their thoughts ■ Build on existing ideas ■ Be brief when stating an idea ■ Strive for Quantity ■ Evaluate ideas ■ Use idea assassins ■ Make judgements (verbal or visual) as ideas are being offered ■ Paraphrase an idea when scribing ■ Use only logical thought processes Improve98 Concept Fan Why use it? The purpose of the concept fan technique is to generate and prioritise possible ideas for solutions to the validated root causes identified in Analyse. Who does it? The tool is designed to be used in a workshop situation comprising stakeholders representing the area most likely impacted by change. It may involve the same or different stakeholders as for earlier stages (Define, Measure) depending on availability and need. The activity usually takes around an hour and will require facilitation by the project team. What does it do? In this context, it is similar to a structured brain storming session, in which you then rank and rate these ideas as a group for assessment and potentially onto further development. How do you do it? Materials required are: ■ Brown paper ■ Bold marker pens ■ Dot stickers Following the steps outlined below will assist delivery of this activity: 1. Present the “story” of the root cause being addressed in the session a. Briefly refer to Define, Measure and Analyse so that everyone in the session knows what they are generating ideas for. It is important to have the critical to quality (CTQ) requirements for the project visible to refer to. 2. Spend 5-10 minutes agreeing on the vision elements for the change required. a. The vision is the desired end state for the change NOT the how to. I.e. if this problem no longer existed and was turned around completely to meet our CTQs what would i. We see? ii. The patients receive in terms of service? iii. Staff feel? iv. The hospital and patients gain? b. How could these vision elements be measured? c. The vision should not prescribe the way to get there d. The facilitator should refer back to the validated root cause as well as the CTQs to ensure that vision would address both 3. Write the vision in a circle in the middle of a piece of brown paper and from there ask participants to suggest ideas of how to achieve this – each idea should be scribed on the brown paper issuing from the vision and each idea should be circled. This will resemble the Why-Because method undertaken in Analyse (refer DMA guide: Section Four: Page 12) Improve WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 99 Once several high level ideas have been scribed around the vision, come back to them one by one and ask the participants to break these down into smaller idea components in order to flesh out the ideas. These components should branch off the bigger idea and also be circled individually. 4. Once the participants have exhausted their ideas, 10 dots per person can be used to rank and rate the ideas against the vision, CTQs and root cause. The team may prefer to use a list of set evaluation criteria against which to rank the ideas (Action 5.5). 5. If you have time in your session, apply some force field analysis (Action 5.4) for the highest rated ideas to discuss some of the enablers and barriers to implementing this idea. 6. Make sure participants: DO: ■ Generate ideas ■ Think outside the square ■ Challenge existing paradigms ■ Understand the problem statement ■ Allow individuals to complete their thoughts ■ Build on existing ideas ■ Be brief when stating an idea ■ Organise, categorise and evaluate only after the session is completed ■ Strive for quantity DON’T: ■ Evaluate ideas ■ Use idea assassins ■ Make judgements, verbal or visual, as ideas are being offered ■ Paraphrase an individual’s idea when scribing ■ Dominate the session ■ Search for the right answer ■ Use only logical thought processes It is important to communicate the outcome of this session as well as the process followed and the participants to other stakeholders in order to create an awareness of possible solutions Once consultation has occurred the next step is for the project team to present the chosen ideas along with projected benefits and risks to the Executive Sponsor and the site Executive for discussion and endorsement. This endorsement is vital before the team continue the solution design process. Improve100 Greenfields Mapping Why use it? To formulate a ‘vision’ of the desired state – how the process map should look. Who does it? The tool is designed to be used in a workshop situation comprising of stakeholders representing the area most likely impacted by change. What does it do? Greenfields mapping: ■ Unlocks people from their preoccupation with the “as-is” process ■ Enables creative input ■ Provides a focus around which meaningful roles can be created ■ Moves away from “adding more people/ roles” to fix the problem to changing what is currently done ■ Provides a great platform for introducing lean principles in redesign How do you do it? The activity usually takes around an hour and will require facilitation by the project team. At least 3 facilitation roles are required: The scribe, a facilitator capturing the steps and a “car park attendant” to capture conversation which needs to be revisited outside the session. Materials required are: ■ Brown paper ■ Bold marker pens ■ Post-it notes (ideally different colours) ■ CTQ requirements, the Vision and validated root causes displayed on paper 1. Present the “story” of the root cause being addressed in the session a. Briefly refer to Define, Measure and Analyse so that everyone in the session knows what they are mapping ideas for. It is important to have the critical to quality (CTQ) requirements for the project visible for reference. 2. Spend 5-10 minutes agreeing on the vision elements for the change required a. The vision is the desired end state for the change NOT the how to. I.e: if this problem no longer existed and was turned around completely to meet our CTQs what would i. We see? ii. The patients receive in terms of service? iii. The staff feel? iv. The hospital and patients gain? b. How could these vision elements be measured? c. The vision should not prescribe the way to get there d. The facilitator should refer back to the validated root cause as well as the CTQs to ensure that vision addresses both Improve WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 101 e. For Greenfield mapping it can also be beneficial to have any available best practice processes. However these are best used after the Greenfield mapping as a comparison rather than an input to the session. Once Best Practice is put on the table, stakeholders can either see no reason to do anything else or dismiss it as “not what we do around here”. Either way it can be limiting if introduced too early. Explaining that there has been some research done and that it will be used as a comparison after the mapping should appease those who are stringent best practice advocates. 3. Agree with the group where the new process should start and end so as to cover the root cause area 4. It is not necessary to do a SIPOC as part of preparation for Green field mapping – if you try and do this beforehand then you are already creating boundaries and preconceived opinions about what the process will be and who will do what. 5. The process is quite straightforward: ask the group to suggest each process step in turn from start to finish. The facilitator’s role is to challenge the group to: a. Think out of the box b. To address the root cause c. Not to start redirecting the session onto who will do what (although of course this will need to be addressed shortly) d. Try to keep the energy and willingness to change to the forefront. It is common for people to slip into “ but we can’t do that because” mode e. Address any concerns or negativity early by explaining that pros and cons of the Greenfield process will be discussed as a next step 6. Once there is some agreement on what a new process could look like then it is time to have a high level chat around who might be the appropriate responsible roles for each new activity. The conversation at this point in time should be brief and suggestive rather than committed. Present the “story” of the root cause being addressed in the session. Make sure people know that nothing is cast in stone. The question to ask is “what might be the best role to undertake this step?”. Avoid detailed discussion and potential arguments. It is quite acceptable to leave a blank at this stage. You are still at concept level. Right now it is more about getting people thinking. 7. Finish you session with a “next steps” summary of what the team will be doing with the output of this session, e.g.: a. Reviewing it against the best practice research b. Examining the gaps more in terms of what is currently done and mapped c. Discussing the model with more appropriate stakeholders Improve102 An example of the ‘desired state’ after Greenfield mapping: Old process Improve WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 103 Standard operation procedures New process map JDFs New process Improve104 Improve WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 105 Control This is the final stage of the DMAIC redesign process and involves solution implementation, the ongoing evaluation and monitoring of solutions, succession planning to incorporate changes into core business, and ensuring sustainable and continuous improvement through revisiting DMAI. Control is the longest phase in the process and in some situations will be an ongoing process. The purpose of Control is to ensure the gains and improvements made in the previous stages are not lost; and further improvement opportunities are continually identified. Key Activities: ■ Solution building ■ Solution implementation ■ Solution monitoring and reporting ■ Evaluation of solutions ■ Succession planning/ Solutions review ■ Continuous improvement Overview of Activities Step 1: Solution Building Following the selection and prioritisation of solutions, implementation planning for each solution needs to be considered and completed. The purpose of implementation planning is to document the steps that need to be completed to move a solution concept into implementation. Planning will also outline how these steps will be achieved and their timeline. Comprehensive implementation planning is a vital part of the project to ensure adequate preparation is taken before implementing a change, and issues and risks are managed. See the end of this section for instruction on how to do product planning. Project Implementation Plan A Project Implementation Plan (PIP) is the key overarching document for creating a solution outline. A PIP should include the: required resources, project team (including project leads and executives sponsors), start date, goals, milestones, planned achievements, risks, mitigation strategies and evaluation methods. Teams may have this plan endorsed by the executive sponsor or hospital executive group before proceeding. A number of other documents that may form part of the PIP include the following: See the HSIU team for further explanation and assistance with developing a PIP. Control106 Project Management Documents Project management documents, as explained earlier in the Project Initiation phase, may need to be completed for each solution or project. These documents include a Gantt Chart to detail the tasks that need to be completed and their timing, a risk assessment matrix to highlight key risks and how they will be addressed, a communications plan and a stakeholder map. The purposes of these tools are to identify and document what is needed to implement a solution and how this will be achieved. Refer back to the Project Initiation section to refresh on Gantt Charts, Risk Assessment and Stakeholder Mapping. Governance Structure The governance structure and reporting lines for each project should be developed, clearly documented and endorsed. The solution project structures may be similar to the existing governance structure, or is often modified at this stage to include the additional stream. Strong leadership is a key factor in the success of a project or solution implementation, and should be established before any change occurs. This will clarify escalation channels for the project if faced with non-compliance or performance management issues. Refer back to Project Initiation for more on Governance Structures. Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) KPIs are used as a high level gauge to measure achievements. KPIs help determine if the solution has been successfully implemented, if they are having the desired effect or change, and to ensure they are not creating unintended effects. As a guide, two to three KPIs should be developed for each solution. Components of a good KPI measure include being: ■ Available – the data is readily accessible and preferably electronic ■ Measurable – indicator can be measured ■ Actionable – negative or adverse results can be actioned and addressed In addition, sites may also wish to develop performance indicators, lower level measures to monitor progress. Some examples of KPIs, as indicated by the example Solutions Business Case described earlier in this section, are: Patient Journey Boards KPIs: Case management efficiency ■ Decrease length of hospital stay (LOS) by 0.5 days on 50% of wards implementing the visual discharge management system by April 2010- TOPAS monthly dashboard reported to Strategic Work Party ■ > 50% reduction in time for team member to identify whether a patient is intended for discharge the ■ Next day, pre and post implementation audit See the Director General’s Division intranet site for more about Project Management Control WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 107 Dissemination to patient efficiency ■ 90% multi-day patients report being informed of their EDD prior to the discharge day- pre & post implementation audit ■ >50% reduction in time of response to a simple patient discharge readiness enquiry- pre & post implementation audit Bed Allocation effectiveness ■ Improved ability to predict bed vacancies – improved bed turn over Assign Responsibilities Clearly defining project roles is beneficial to the successful planning of a new process. A tool such as a RASCI is useful to achieve this. The purpose of completing a RASCI is to assign responsibilities to each key task of a project or solution. It helps clarify what is expected of those involved in the process, ensures key stakeholders are involved and informed, and helps to ensure there are no overlaps or gaps. Completing a RASCI involves allocating roles to each key task: R = Responsible for completing the task A = Accountable for the outcomes of the task. S = Provides Support resources C = Should be Consulted for information or advice. I = Must be Informed of results of the task. Figure 9: An example RASCI ActivityChief ExecutiveExecutive DirectorSteering GroupProgram AdviserFacility LeadClinical LeadProject officer Instigating the Four Hour Rule Program C I R A S Monitoring progress of the Program S A R Approving funding and resources A R I S Implementing the Program Achieving Program benefits Change Management Change management has been discussed earlier in this guide. During the Implementation phase it is important to revisit your change management strategies and determine if any additions or changes are required. This will ensure best adoption, support and sustainability of the project. See Appendix D to read more about Change Management Control108 Business Cases Your project team may need to develop a business case for the project or solution. A business case is a tool commonly used to gain approval, endorsement, or funding for a project or solution. It outlines the need for change and the proposed benefits, as well as detailing how this change will occur. See the end of this section for an example of a business case. Conducting a Pilot Conducting a pilot is a useful method to test and demonstrate the proposed benefits of a solution. A pilot will allow the project team to assess if the proposed solutions achieve the desired effect. It also allows an opportunity for necessary alterations to a project before full scale implementation and investment. A pilot may involve implementing changes to a specific ward, department or specialty. Considerations of when to conduct a pilot include when the cost of implementing the solution is high, if the change is hard to reverse, if there is potential for wide spread or unintentional consequences, if the scope is large or complex and if there is substantial change to roles or training required. See the end of this section for more on how to conduct a pilot. Step 2: Solution Implementation Once project implementation planning has been completed, the next step is implementation of solutions. This should be done in accordance with the PIP and in the timeframes specified by the Gantt chart. One aspect of solution implementation may include the development of standard operating procedures. Develop Standard Operating Procedures Standard Operating Procedures (SOPS) are instructions to achieve uniformity of performance in an area of work. SOPS ensure consistency of processes, procedures and documentation, reducing variation. Any new or modified roles or processes should have developed SOPs to guide staff. SOPs are also beneficial for training staff new to the area or process and assist with keeping the project’s gains sustainable by avoiding a relapse to the old process. Control WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 109 Table 5: Example standard operating procedures – provision of manual handling training to hospital staff Training Provision ■ Requirement for Manual Handling Training/Competency is based on the level of risk of the person’s occupation. ■ Training can be completed annually, biannually, or on commencement only. ■ Training can also be provided in different formats, i.e. Online, Lecture, Generic, Ward/Department Based, or on the job. Trainers ■ Training may be provided by a Manual Handling Consultant from the OSH Department, a Ward/Department Based Manual Handling Trainer (with current competence) or a contracted level 4 trainer. Trainer: Participant Ratio ■ 1 manual handling trainer will be provided by the OSH department for all organised DNAMER and PNE orientation and refresher sessions up to 12 participants. ■ Where greater than 12 participants are booked into a single session a 2nd trainer may be required based on a risk assessment. ■ Factors included in the risk assessment are: ■ Tasks involved ■ Number of participants practicing at once ■ Scope to be able to split the group ■ Trainers’ discretion. ■ If a 2nd trainer is required, as much as possible the trainer will be from the OSH department. On some occasions the 2nd trainer may need to come from the pool of manual handling trainer assessors within the hospital. Facilities and Equipment ■ Training facilities should be adequate to comfortably accommodate the number of participants booked into a session. All equipment should be in good working order. Whenever possible electric height adjustable beds should be utilised. ■ Some equipment may need to be borrowed from the hospital for training, i.e. HoverMatts Step 3: Solution Monitoring and Reporting Project monitoring is a key step in the Control phase. The purpose of continuously monitoring a project is to assess the status of the project’s deliverables, achievements, goals, milestones and risks. Periodic reporting is also beneficial for identifying and documenting issues that arise. This step involves developing a regular reporting structure which documents the progress of the project and has clear lines of accountability up to and including the Executive of the hospital. The Project should have governance at the operational level (a project control group) and the ability to report up for decision making to the executive body. It is preferable to use an existing group for this purpose, such as a steering committee or control group, rather than establishing a new group. Control110 Control reports are a common method used to track and document progress. Control reports are a periodic document that may contain: ■ Deliverables achieved or planned ■ Issues and risks ■ Risk mitigation strategies ■ KPIs ■ Budget See the end of this section for an example of a control report. Step 4: Succession Planning/Solutions Review Succession planning serves to ensure the solutions that have been implemented are sustained. It is an important step as it ensures that the benefits of each solution continue to be delivered, and that the new processes, systems and roles become a part of the site’s culture and core business in time. Succession planning needs to be undertaken in conjunction with ongoing solution review. Succession planning and implementation ensures that: ■ The benefits of each solution continue to be delivered ■ In time the new processes, systems and roles become part of the site’s culture In order to ensure the solution carries on, it is important to consider the following: ■ There is an identified process owner who is well briefed on the solution and CSR methodology, and risks and issues ■ DMAI processes and materials are easy to replicate and accessible (eg. Process maps, RASCI charts, dashboards). ■ Updating the project RASCI to develop an operational RASCI to reflect new process owners and accountabilities ■ All new deliverables have been designed, built and implemented eg. Processes, tools (forms, systems, IT). ■ Roles and responsibilities are clear and in place ■ KPIs have been agreed on and are being measured ■ The solution has been reviewed and issues resolved ■ Governance, reporting and support structures are reviewed ■ Review training/skill requirements and developing plans to address if necessary ■ Review Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) (for roles and processes) and updating these if necessary (see Step 4 of Improve for more about SOPs). See the end of this section for further information on solutions review and an exam- ple of a dashboard Step 5: Continuous Improvement The DMAIC redesign process follows a circular path. It is not designed for once-off projects that are finished when the Control phase is ‘over’. Rather, the process should be continually evaluated and revisited to build upon improvements. Control WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 111 Lessons Learnt Documenting lessons learnt is a valuable exercise that can be completed at any stage of the DMAIC redesign process. Lessons learnt should be documented, often in a lessons learnt log, and may consider what process, methods or strategies did or did not work well. This will allow future projects to avoid making the same mistakes and to build on the successes of past experiences. It is important at the close of a project to discuss and document lessons learned. An example of such an activity for a change program is below. A signed-off solution does not equal an implemented solution ■ As much detailed project management needs to go into implementation as went into Define, Measure and Analyse ■ The person signing off the solution should be accountable for reporting to the Hospital Executive Team on its KPIs and benefits ■ Does the person responsible for the solution’s implementation (the “owner”) have dedicated project time away from their operational / clinical load? Implementation is hard and needs time. Executive ownership of solution outcomes is they key. This means: ■ The Executive Owners of the solution report back to their Hospital ED and their Executive Group regularly on progress against relevant criteria including time, quality and risk ■ The Hospital Executive Director holds their Executive team to account for the solution delivering their expected outcomes on time, so that this rigour around deliverables can be mirrored on the floor. Get the role of the Project team lead right ■ Needs to have access to decision makers ■ It is not the ‘Solution Implementer’ nor should the team be held accountable for the success or failure of solutions ■ Can offer valuable project, change and process management guidance to the solutions owners but cannot make the latter take up the offer The more “churn” at senior level in the facility, the harder sustainability will be, and: ■ The more plans and clarity on roles and responsibilities will become vital ■ The less you should be relying on individuals to push solutions across the line Becoming a “learning organisation” ■ Have you used the project as an opportunity to embed competencies of project and change management and to build a core change team on which you can draw for the next challenge? Or will you be starting from scratch looking for new methodologies, new people and new buy-in to change? Revisit DMAI As the DMAIC redesigning process is a continuous process, teams should consider the need to revisit the earlier stages at some point in the future. Revisiting the process will allow the team to further build on the gains achieved and address any issues that have arisen from the new process. Control112 ■ Consider if any training or education is required before implementation ■ Evaluation will be an ongoing task both in Implementation and Control. It is necessary to track solution progress and assess its impacts, both negative and positive in a timely manner to avoid major project disruptions ■ The project tools including the Gantt chart, risk assessment matrix and the communications plan should be reviewed periodically to document any changes ■ Do not underestimate the amount of effort required for this final stage. It often requires more time and enthusiasm to sustain any change, improvements or gains, than what is needed for the first four phases ■ It may be necessary to revisit the earlier steps of the DMAIC process to achieve continuous improvement ■ Executive and clinical leadership are critical in driving sustainability and to ensure recommendations from solution reviews and evaluations are implemented ■ At some point the project needs to end, and the change becomes a part of everyday practice.
