Hints and Tips Control WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 113 Control: Tools and Resources Undertaking Product Planning Why use it? It enables the team and the Executive to understand solution implementation considerations. Who does it? The project team What does it do? It is a way of developing a simple work stream breakdown in a small group. How do you do it? To undertake product planning the following is required: ■ The Executive endorsed concept and/or Greenfield process ■ Brown paper ■ Post it notes ■ Bold markers This activity can be facilitated by one person and is best run with the project team rather than a broader group of stakeholders. The facilitator should follow the steps below: 1. With reference to the chosen solution and vision for the solution, the team brainstorm all the things they think are needed to produce the overall solution. The facilitator writes these down on individual post it notes and places them vertically on the piece of brown paper – in no particular order. The emphasis is on things rather than verbs so that the plan is deliverable-driven rather than ongoing activity-driven 2. Once suggestions have been exhausted, the facilitator works with the team to order the products in the order they need to be delivered 3. The team discuss whether the list is exhaustive, if there are gaps or if there is duplication (i.e. – is one thing a subset of another rather than discrete) 4. The next step is to allocate a timeline to each product development. The paper then starts to somewhat resemble a Gantt chart 5. The final step is to go back to each product and commence a RASCI for each product. As a minimum the R and the A columns should be completed 6. Post workshop, the team lead can reproduce this high level plan in any format – excel, PIP Microsoft project, Prince. 7. From this document various work streams can develop their own specific work plans but the team lead and project team can monitor progress across all streams using this master plan. Control114 Example Product Implementation Plan Week 1 Week 2 Week 3 Week 4 Week 5 New process Roles Criteria Change documentation Data measures Training Control WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 115 Example of a Solutions Business Case [Facility] Hospital [Solution name] Patient Journey Boards, Four Hour Rule Program SOLUTION TITLE: Patient Journey Boards SUBMITTED BY: John Green DATE: 20 November 2009 APPROVALS EXECUTIVE/REGIONAL DIRECTOR ¨ SIGNED: DATE: SECTION A: INTRODUCTION Proposed solution Briefly state what the proposed solution is. The solution itself may be broader than what funding is being sought for. For example: To use visual management in the form of a white board with a magnetic “traffic light” coded system to share information pertinent to discharge planning. This communication tool will be updated regularly, can be viewed by all ward team members “at a glance” and will be a focal point for effective reporting on progress in relation to the shared team goal of safe, timely hospital discharge. The need (opportunity statement) Should address these questions: ■ What problem/issue is being addressed? ■ How extensive is the problem? ■ What is wrong or not working? ■ What is the impact or ‘pain’ on our patients (and families); organisation; employees? ■ When, where and how often does the problem occur? The opportunity statement should also include evidence of the root cause of the problem, justifying the solution. Where possible, the evidence should include supporting data collated during the DMAI phases. If this is part of an existing solution, include audit information. For example: Magnetic whiteboards on each inpatient ward will improve discharge procedural efficiencies which will be pivotal in enabling beds to be available for patients requiring admissions at Sir Charles Gairdner Hospital. Data analysis has shown that when beds are available, time to allocate a bed is 0 minutes in almost all cases whereas when there is no bed available, there is a 1 hour and 59 minute delay to patient flow. Root causes identified through the program for these delays were focal to discharge-planning: 1. Lack of responsibility to take ownership for discharge 2. Focus on clinical care, rather than discharge 3. Discharge decision makers were not always present on the ward 4. Discharge processes varied from ward to ward, making it difficult for staff who work across multiple areas Control116 Solutions objectives/goals The proposed solution should have a small number of high-level, overarching objectives. List these in priority order. Each objective should be specific, measurable, attainable, relevant and time bound. For example: Efficiencies surrounding case management, discharge preparedness of patients, and bed allocation through implementing the visual discharge management system will be apparent by April 2010. This aligns strongly with strategic objectives of decreasing ward access block to improve flow from the Emergency Department. This goal will be more attainable on wards where efficiencies surrounding discharge planning are prone to team communication delays. The communication medium itself however may not expedite discharge where medical recovery dictates a patients’ hospitalisation duration. Operational policy of the visual management system will include a designation of daily responsibility for a team member to disseminate the discharge plan and estimated date (EDD) to patients and/or their carers. There will be a target of 90% of multi-day patients reporting being informed of their EDD prior to their day of discharge by April 2010. Intent is to measure baseline and repeat following implementation. The average number of times a nurse is interrupted for patient information should reduce 30% by April 2010. It is likely some enquiries will continue when specific nursing detail is sought. Scope Should address these questions: ■ What components of the organisation are included? What, if anything, is outside the boundary of this project? ■ Under what constraints must the team work? ■ Timeframe for solution For example: The scope is for all inpatients requiring a discharge plan. Components of the organisation within scope include all inpatient wards, nursing, medical, allied health and support services teams. Out of scope are the Emergency Department and Intensive Care units as discharge planning will be primarily directed by the medical practitioner. Constraints include ■ Minimal use of extra resources ■ Limits of existing physical area and accommodation of additional information to existing board content ■ Education and significant cultural change for staff The concept has already been successfully introduced on 2 wards with a recent productive ward initiative. Time frame for implementation is proposed for January 2010 pending funding and delivery of required resources. Control WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 117 Option evaluation Identify and briefly describe the options that were considered, including the “do nothing” option. Why is this solution seen as the preferred way to address the problem? For example: Option: “Do Nothing” Status Quo- Rejected as viable option as the earlier discharge on day of discharge will not occur and current access block to inpatient beds will continue. In addition, ward culture focuses primarily on daily care rather than discharge planning as reflected by current utilization of the ward communication board. These span over 40 square meters at SCGH however a snapshot found only 2% of that space recorded discharge plans. A dedicated resource is considered essential to making discharge planning a higher priority for staff. Other communication systems including formal team meetings, documentation in integrated notes and care plans will be reviewed. Whilst there remains a need to retain these communication channels, patient journey boards offer both a focus on discharge planning and a far more efficient utilisation of valuable staff time everyday. The “at a glance system” will release burden of ■ Finding integrated notes and awaiting availability for use ■ Filtering through written integrated care notes to find pertinent discharge information ■ Waiting for telephone access, paging and interrupting colleagues. Interdependencies What other programs/initiatives locally or Statewide have an interdependency with this solution? Outline how the solution will maximise benefits from these. How does this solution fit in with the facility/Area Health Service/WA Health strategic priorities (including endorsed Models of Care)? IMPORTANT: Please specify if this solution depends on the successful implementation of other project/s planned at your site. For example: This project is dependent on the Estimated time of Discharge Solution Implementation. The EDD Project will negotiate agreement with Consultants and Medical staff to nominate the EDD on admission and changes to the current discharge form will document the EDD. These two milestones will need to occur before the roll out of the Patient Journey Boards Use of a visual management system for discharge has interdependencies with other local strategies being implemented under the Four Hour Rule. These include ■ An Indirect influence on ED streaming by availing ward beds more efficiently ■ Four Hour Rule Ward Leadership initiatives are likely to benefit from a central team focused visual communication tool It will provide a focus and efficiency in sharing discharge information- amber traffic light of “near ready” will identify priority cases for more detailed focus during team board meetings. Control118 Solution cost Outline total solution cost for the duration of the solution. For example: The total cost of this solution is $15,032. SECTION B: PROJECT MANAGEMENT AND IMPLEMENTATION Program/project deliverables Provide a high-level program / project plan, including key activities, milestones and resources dedicated to developing the plan and rolling-out the solution. This can form the basis for a more detailed plan if the solution is approved. For example use of a Gantt Chart. Governance Indicate how the solution implementation will be managed and reported to the Program Management Office within the hospital facility and to existing governance structures. For example: The implementation of this initiative will be the responsibility of Mrs Smith, Project Officer on the Four Hour Rule Program. Mrs. Smith will report progress to lead of the Discharge initiative, Dr Brown. Dr Brown will report on progress, and interdependencies with other initiatives to the Strategic working group. Stakeholders Indicate key stakeholders that should be consulted / involved for this specific solution. This can later form part of a more detailed stakeholder mapping log if the solution is approved. For example: Stakeholder engagement: ■ CNS and Allied Health initial engagement positive ■ Small work party formed from Discharge stream & combining existing work of Productive ward ■ Solutions fair display to wider hospital team ■ Ward-based education sessions planned Change planning and management Indicate how the solution will incorporate key components that will be used to manage and sustain change successfully. This can later form part of a more detailed change management plan if the solution is approved. For example: Change management strategies include: ■ Appointing process ownership to ward CNS ■ Wide stakeholder involvement and education ■ Display of objectives, baseline and target achievements by ward ■ Integration with interdependencies such as daily team board meetings to sustain the change process and embed the work practice. Control WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 119 Risk management Highlight key risks associated with the solution and mitigation strategies. This information should form the basis for a more detailed risk management log if the solution is approved. For example: Risk management strategies planned are: ■ Extensive collaboration with ward board users to ensure concept matches ward- specific needs ■ Investigation and trial of resources to ensure user and process-friendly options are sourced ■ Regular team reviews will identity any compliance issues relating to updating of discharge information ■ Responsibility and guidelines for use will be incorporated into staff orientation processes SECTION C: MONITORING AND EVALUATION – MEASURABLE BENEFITS AND KPIs Name and description of measurable benefit What are the measurable benefits to be achieved by your solution? How will it benefit the patient? For example: ■ Reduced length of hospital stay due to more efficient case management ■ Improved discharge effectiveness due to patient and family preparation for discharge ■ More effective bed allocation based on matching of expected date of discharge to actual discharge date Name and description of key performance indicators (KPIs) Please complete the following table with the key performance indicators (KPIs) that you will use to measure the benefits and success of this solution. The KPI/s should reflect and quantify the benefits that your solution will achieve. For example: Case management efficiency ■ Decrease length of hospital stay (LOS) by 0.5 days on 50% of wards implementing the visual discharge management system by April 2010- TOPAS monthly dashboard reported to Strategic Work Party ■ > 50% reduction in time for team member to identify whether a patient is intended for discharge the next day, pre and post implementation audit Rationale for using the KPIs What is the purpose of using this KPI? For example, does the KPI reflect an improved outcome for the patient or the staff? Is it possible to easily measure and quantify the desired changes over time? Control120 For example: Visual management will readily identify barriers to discharge, allow proactive case management and result in a reduction in LOS. Mapping of time taken to communicate discharge information, pre and post initiative implementation, will measure effectiveness of the journey board in conveying information to staff and patients. Source of KPI data Where will your KPI data come from? Ideally the source of data will already exist and can be extracted in an automated fashion. For example: Reduction in ALOS by 0.5 days on 50% of wards implementing the visual discharge management system by April 2010, can be measured from TOPAS reporting. Who will obtain and produce the KPI data Identify who will perform this function, and confirm that it is able to fit into the person’s job / project role. For example: Dr Jones will coordinate members of the Discharge Initiative Stream to obtain case management and patient dissemination efficiency audits. How often will it be measured What are realistic intervals at which to obtain your KPI data? Too frequent can be too resource intensive; too infrequent may hinder your ability to detect and respond to positive or negative changes. Analysis and response to KPI data Who in your project team will monitor the data being produced? Who will respond to any positive or negative changes? Will action need to be escalated at any point – if so, when? What if changes need to be made to your solution’s strategy? If so, who will plan and approve? For example: Discharge Clinical lead, Dr Jones will report and respond to data being produced and escalate any changes required to the Strategic Work Party Group as identified. Control WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 121 Conducting a pilot Why use it? To better understand the impacts of solution implementation and obtain feedback from: ■ Patient ■ Vendors/Suppliers ■ Process/Workers ■ Internal Technology/Existing Systems Who does it? The project team. What does it do? Conducting a pilot gives the opportunity to: ■ Reduce the risk of failure by identifying areas of potential failure ■ Increase buy-in ■ Validate and refine cost – benefit estimates ■ Perform adjustments ■ Evaluate the effectiveness of measures used to monitor the improvement ■ Test the validity of the solution ■ Better understand the impacts of the solution and obtain feedback from the patient and staff How do you do it? The basic methodology behind a pilot is the Plan-Do-Study-Act cycle: ■ Plan a test ■ Try it ■ Observe results ■ Act on what was learned Factors to consider: ■ Cost is high or resources are currently unavailable ■ Credibility for the design needs to be established ■ Difficult to reverse solution consequences and/or results ■ Potential far-reaching and/or unintended consequences ■ Scope of the design is complex or large ■ Substantial training required ■ Training and substantial changes to job designs Control122 Example: Root Cause Delay to receiving diagnostic results has been identified as a root cause in delay to admission and delay to discharge home. ■ Data shows that Radiology and Pathology staff process requests efficiently after they are received. Further data shows a significant delay in doctors reviewing the results once available. Data shows this ranges from 38 mins to 3 hours with a median delay of 1.78 hours. A potential solution is the use of a messaging alert system to let doctors know when their results are available for review. Overview of the Pilot: The system will be piloted in the hospital. The pilot will be conducted with two medical specialty and two surgical specialty teams and the Emergency Department and will involve approximately 100 medical staff. Pilot Scope: ■ Baseline data will be collected on all radiology and pathology requests for two weeks prior to the pilot. ■ The Messaging system will be linked to all nominated medical staff’s pager or work mobile phone. ■ The message alerts during the pilot will include a results ready message and also an urgent flag if any results are significantly abnormal. ■ The system will track the response of the medical staff and correlate this with the time results were reviewed. ■ Data collected will include tracking response, feedback from doctors on the benefits and issues with the system and any functionality issues with either ICT Platform or current paging system. ■ This data will be analysed to evaluate potential benefits, cost estimates for implementation in hospitals. Control WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 123 Solutions review Why use it? It is vital that from pilot, through implementation and post implementation, regular reviews of solutions are conducted so that: ■ Stakeholders continue to feel involved in the solution; the to enhance commitment to the changes is taken advantage of ■ Gives staff the confidence that they’ll be consulted on how it is going ■ It remains “ live” and on the agenda of the areas involved ■ Is an opportunity to revisit the solution and assess its strengths, weaknesses, impact on KPIs, and risks and issues, and how to further improve it, and to look at what solutions are: – Not working – Not being followed – Working well and could be extended – Disliked – Liked Who does it? Representatives from across the scope of the solution should be invited and the session can be facilitated by 1-2 people. These people do not necessarily need to be from the project team – as it is possible that the latter may no longer be operating as a CSR project team anymore; but it is important that as many stakeholders of the solution are present as possible. What does it do? Scheduled solutions reviews are a good way to gather feedback on the newly developed solution. How do you do it? These sessions generally last for 45 minutes to 1 hour. Preparation: ■ Identify all stakeholders in the new solution’s process (SIPOC) ■ Invite representatives of each to a scheduled review workshop approximately 4 weeks post solution launch ■ Ensure all stakeholders in the new solution are aware of the workshop and its date so they can feedback to those attending ■ Book a venue with space for interaction ■ Invite the solution’s Executive Sponsor to introduce the session ■ Appoint an independent facilitator (i.e. not involved in the solution) ■ Objectives – To reflect back on how the new solution is going and to identify how well it is working and any areas needing to be further refined Control124 ■ Agenda – Introduce the solution, the key data points, and the root causes being resolved. – Identify positive outcomes of the new solution (Use Affinity mapping for this process). – Identify any negative outcomes of the new solution – Analyse the sources of the negative outcomes – Agree on actions to resolve these sources – Agree on a way forward Affinity mapping: revisit the Analyse section for more about Affinity Mapping. ■ Affinity Mapping allows a team to creatively generate a large number of issues and organise natural groupings to understand the essence of the problem – Ask group to brainstorm ideas and record each on a separate post-it note (positive then negative); – Again in silence, sort the ideas into groups; – It is OK for some notes to stand alone, and for others to be duplicated between two groups if necessary. – For each grouping, capture a word or phase that describes the central theme as the header. – Ask staff to vote on which are the most positive/most negative ■ What is the output? – A set of positive and negative impacts for further analysis – Some issues will appear in the positive and negative groups (a clue that implementation has been variable in quality) ■ Take the negative issues from the Affinity Mapping, and understand what is causing them: – Design issues – i.e. the solution is fundamentally flawed, a key process step is missing, the new forms don’t capture the required information, the new kit doesn’t work – Implementation issues – i.e. Staff don’t know what to do, compliance is patchy etc ■ The solution team’s approach to each type of problem is very different: – Design issues need rework to key solution products i.e. new forms – Solution issues need to be dealt with via change management, training and sometimes performance management ■ Actions around both need to be agreed, owned and planned. ■ It is often necessary to hold a follow up scheduled review once the issues identified in the workshop have been addressed. Solutions review checklist: ■ Do we have a vision for the solution? ■ Does it clearly align to the validated problem and overall change vision? ■ Will we be able to measure when we have got there and are our measures balanced (do they extend to the patient, are there qualitative as well as quantitative indicators) ? ■ Does each solution area have a plan outlining what needs to be produced, when and by whom to develop a robust solution? ■ Is it documented with accountabilities, deliverables and timelines? ■ Are you building an effective and sustainable solution? ■ Is everyone clear on what they will have to do/change? Control WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 125 ■ Is it clear how the solution will measure? ■ Is there a start date? ■ Is everyone clear on what will happen on the start date? ■ Are there dates for review and feedback? ■ Is there a plan for how the solution skills will be identified and transferred? Other points to consider when conducting a solutions review session: ■ Use of Dashboards to track effectiveness of solution ■ Monitor implementation progress ■ Include formal review points in your project implementation plan ■ Check the progress of activities against the plan ■ Review performance regularly and at the stipulated review points (against pre-agreed measures) ■ Inform and escalate about developments to team members, Executive sponsors ■ Get the facts and figures, analyse causes and amend if required ■ Identify, agree and delegate new actions as appropriate Much emphasis was placed on stakeholder engagement during the Define, Measure and Analyse stages. Improve and Control should continue to engage those who have developed the solution and who are most impacted by the changes. Control WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 131Control132 Control WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 133 Appendix A: Stakeholder Management Stakeholder Management Stakeholder management involves the identification of people or organisations that have an interest in the project processes, outputs or outcomes/ benefits, and planning for how their involvement will be managed on an ongoing basis. It may be done very quickly for a small project, whereas a large and/ or more complex project will require a formal stakeholder analysis and ongoing monitoring and review of progress including the development of a stakeholder and communication plan (Department of Health WA). It is important that you have your stakeholder identification plans prepared and reviewed by the project team, to ensure no one has been left out. You will also need your communication strategy (see Appendix B: Communication Management) so you are aware of what communication approach to take with each stakeholder. People are more likely to engage with and support your project if they feel like that have been included and engaged with themselves. To help identify all the stakeholders in a project, consider ■ What functions or people might be affected by the project’s activities or outcomes? ■ Who contributes resources- people, space, time, tools and money – to the project? Who should I engage? ■ Engage people at all levels, across a range of disciplines ■ At the management level, analyse who will be champions and who will be blockers to your project – this is vital to how you communicate with them ■ You need support from across the board – so don’t forget people at the operational level of the organisation who know how things ‘really’ work ■ Identify those people who are followers and who are leaders How should I engage them? ■ Develop a robust communication plan including each stakeholder – appeal to their interests by considering ‘what’s in it for them’ to get them on board Stakeholder analysis When conducting the stakeholder analysis, you need to consider two main aspects of stakeholder engagement – their level of influence (low, medium or high) and whether they are an opponent, follower or enthusiast. This will provide a clear picture of all the groups and individuals that need to be involved, helps reduce the negative impact of mistakenly leaving stakeholders out, and also informs the best way to communicate with them. Different groups will perceive the project differently. This may be because of differences in organisational culture, for example, between emergency department people and those who work in the wards. It may also be because they will be influenced by the project in different ways. Because stakeholder interests vary, their expectations are also likely to differ. Stakeholder communication needs will also vary depending on their level of influence and support for the project. One of the critical tasks in this phase is to meld stakeholders’ expectations into a coherent and manageable set of project objectives. Appendices134 Appendix B: Communication Management Good communications management is an essential component to organisations’ business development. This is especially the case for when a change occurs – if stakeholders are not adequately engaged you risk losing their support and undermining the success of the proposed changes. The communication planning tool provided in this guide is an important tool to guide good communication management. To ensure your approach your communication management in the best way possible, consider the following steps: 1. Be clear of your overall change objectives; and how you would like these to be communicated to the rest of the organisation. What would you like to achieve? Perhaps just an awareness of the change is required; or rather investment and support from key staff members is required in order for the change to occur. 2. Be aware of all the types of people who will need to be aware of your message. It is helpful to revisit your SIPOC or stakeholder analysis tools here. 3. Match your objectives to the people who will be receiving your message. What part of the message does each stakeholder need? What level of engagement in the message is necessary? 4. Once your stakeholders and the type of message they need is identified; you need to think about the method of communicating with them. Utilise all resources available; for example: a. Emails b. Newsletters c. Regular staff meetings d. Posters e. Launch events f. Websites/Intranet 5. Plan your message. Different stakeholders will require different elements of the message. Think about what they need to know, how frequently and what their preferred channel of communication is likely to be. 6. Get feedback from all stakeholders to ensure that everyone you need to be reached has received your message, and that they interpreted the message as you intended so. This will help to inform future communications tasks. (Mind Tools 2013) Appendices WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 135 Appendix C: Risk Management Risk management Risk management is “the culture, processes and structures that are directed towards realising potential opportunities whilst managing adverse effects” (Health Risk Management Policy Framework, 2005, p. 2). Risks are characterised in the following way: ■ They are an uncertain event ■ They are “in the future” ■ If they impact (happen), they become a problem/issue and are no longer a risk ■ Risk and opportunity are two sides of the same coin ■ You can accept (live with) a risk, and realise a positive opportunity. Risk has two components: ■ Likelihood – how likely is it to occur ■ Impact – What is the expected loss if it does The two components of risk are analysed by rating how likely it is to occur, and the expected impact if it does. ■ What do you consider to be the top 5 risks for your Project at its outset? ■ Map these risks on the Risk/Probability Matrix. The risk management process Figure 10 Identify the risk Analyse the risk Develop plans to reduce or eliminate the risk Monitor the risk Risks should be identified at the outset of a redesign program and documented in the Risk Log. As further risks are identified during the DMAIC stages these should also be added to the risk log and managed at a project or program level. The risk log is a live document and will be critical through the Define stage and through Implementation - you may want to review the project risk log on a weekly/fortnightly basis. When reviewing the risk log, check that the responses to the identified risks are still effective/ appropriate. As circumstances change, things that were previously risks may no longer be a problem, or new risks may emerge. The project environment also needs to be constantly monitored to identify if/ when new risks emerge. Do not assume that one person can monitor all the potential risks. The best project mangers seek feedback from other people to monitor risk. For example, speak with the team and stakeholders frequently to ask: ■ How do you think the project’s going? ■ What do you think the main risks and issues are with this stage in the project? By doing this on a regular basis, risks will be able identified that others may not have ever been aware of. Appendices136 Responding to Risk When it comes to treating risks, you need to consider: ■ What you can do before the risk occurs, to either prevent the risk occurring or minimise its impact ■ What you will do in the event that the risk occurs, to mitigate damage There are 5 approaches to take when addressing identified risks: 1. Prevention: terminate the risk - by doing things differently and thus removing the risk where it is feasible to do so. Ie. countermeasures to either stop the problem occurring or prevent it impacting. 2. Reduction: treat the risk - take action to control it in some way where the actions either reduce the likelihood of the risk developing or limit impact to acceptable levels. 3. Transference: specialist form of risk reduction whereby risk is passed to third party via insurance or penalty clause, such that it is no longer an issue for project. This is not suitable for all risks. 4. Acceptance: tolerate risk - perhaps because nothing can be done at a reasonable cost to mitigate it or the likelihood or impact of the risk are at an acceptable level. 5. Contingency: actions planned and organised to come into force as and when the risk occurs. Appendices WA Health Clinical Service Redesign Handbook | A Training Guide 137Appendices Appendix D: Change management Change Management Change management is the way an organisation implements changes to the business environment in a way that ensures a smooth transition. Changes in organisations will have impacts and effects on staff and processes. To help minimise those impacts and effects, it is necessary to use change management principles to help increase positive reaction to change. This includes planning and documentation of all change management strategies. Good change management helps: ■ Employees to have a solid understanding of why change is happening ■ Employees to engage in both the solution and the change ■ To identify and deal with resistance ■ Senior leaders to demonstrate their own and the organization’s commitment to change ■ Changes to be less painful to the organization and to the employees ■ To increase the probability of meeting project objectives ■ The organization to build a history of successful change, creating a better culture for accepting future changes. John Kotter’s (Kotter International 2011) eight step change model is one theory which can be used to guide your site’s change management. The steps involved are: create a sense of urgency, form a guiding coalition, create a vision, communicate the vision, empower others to act on the vision, create quick wins, build on the change, and institutionalise the change. 1. Create urgency All staff affected by the change need to realise the need and therefore the motivation for change. Promote the potential risks and/ or threats to the organisation if the change does not occur, and the benefits if the change does occur. 2. Form a powerful coalition Change needs to come from leaders – from any level within the organisation. Find people who will champion your cause to change, to help other staff get on board. 3. Create a vision for change You need to be able to explain your vision for change succinctly and convincingly. Link your vision for the change to what people already value or respect in the organisation ie. Better patient care. 4. Communicate the vision You need to incorporate your change vision into every aspect of the way you do business and at every opportunity. Use every opportunity to communicate the change – not just at specially called meetings. 5. Remove obstacles You need to identify potential barriers to change – people, job roles, or structures within the work place which do not facilitate or support the change you need to see happen. Identify these potential barriers and take steps to eliminate or manage them. Check in with staff from whom you’ve gained support for your change, and make sure they are on the right track to promoting your change. 138 6. Create short term wins Gain support and investment in your change by identifying a short- term target that is fail-proof which you can use to celebrate the benefits of the change. Having staff see the benefits of the change, and recognising and rewarding staff involved with the small victory, will give you the support and justification to tackle the bigger, more challenging, barriers to change. 7. Build on the change Don’t become complacent if the change appears to have been established. Set a long time goal or milestone that will undoubtedly prove that the change is here to stay. If not, you risk reverting back to old ways. Continuously check in with staff and your performance indicators to make sure things are constantly improving. 8. Anchor the changes in corporate culture Ensure that the changes are embedded in the organisation’s values and culture. Change management is a continuous activity and involves constantly checking in with key employees to make sure they are still on board with the new processes, ensuring that job description profiles are created to embed the change into the organisation; and that, if key change champions leave the workplace, they are replaced by someone equally able to carry on the change legacy.
